narrative_blocks.header: Led 100 settlers from Cambridge to found Hartford
narrative_blocks.body: In the summer of 1636, Hooker led a company of one hundred men, women, and children from Newtown (Cambridge) to found Hartford — a hundred miles through wilderness with no path, guided only by a compass. They drove 160 cattle before them, cleared thickets with axes, built causeways from felled trees, rested in shady groves, and ate wild strawberries. They made the journey in a fortnight.

narrative_blocks.header: Called the light of the western churches
narrative_blocks.body: Hooker was universally regarded by his contemporaries as the foremost preacher in New England — Cotton Mather called him "the light of the western churches."

narrative_blocks.header: His dynasty: Yale presidents, VP, Medal of Honor
narrative_blocks.body: Thomas Hooker's descendants across fifteen generations include some of the most consequential figures in American religious, political, and intellectual history.

narrative_blocks.header: His democracy was covenantal, not Jeffersonian
narrative_blocks.body: Hooker's political theology was not proto-democracy but covenantal ecclesiology applied to civil life — the Puritan doctrine that a gathered community must consent to its own governance.

narrative_blocks.header: He moderated the Hutchinson trial, then left
narrative_blocks.body: In 1637, Hooker served as one of two moderators over the three-week inquiry into Anne Hutchinson's antinomian doctrines — which culminated in her excommunication and banishment from Massachusetts Bay. Having completed his role, Hooker returned to Hartford before the trial concluded.

narrative_blocks.header: His 1638 sermon framed Connecticut's constitution
narrative_blocks.body: On May 31, 1638, preaching before the General Court assembled to frame Connecticut's constitution, Hooker declared: "The foundation of authority is laid in the free consent of the people." The Fundamental Orders of Connecticut, adopted January 14, 1639 — often called the world's first written democratic constitution — grew from this sermon.

narrative_blocks.header: Fiske: more than any man, democracy's father
narrative_blocks.body: John Fiske wrote that the Fundamental Orders of Connecticut marked "the beginnings of American democracy, of which Thomas Hooker deserves more than any other man to be called the father." He added that "the government of the United States today is in lineal descent more nearly related to that of Connecticut than to that of any of the other thirteen colonies."

narrative_blocks.header: Winthrop called him peerless in New England
narrative_blocks.body: John Winthrop wrote of Hooker that "for piety, prudence, wisdom, zeal, learning, and what else might make a minister of the Gospel, he might be compared with men of greatest note." Henry Whitfield, who knew the foremost English divines, wrote after meeting Hooker: "I did not think there had been such a man on the earth."

narrative_blocks.header: Dying, he said: I go to receive mercy
narrative_blocks.body: When a weeping bystander told him, "Sir, you are going to receive the reward of all your labours," Thomas Hooker replied: "Brother, I am going to receive mercy!" He died July 7, 1647, of an epidemic sickness, at Hartford.

narrative_blocks.header: No portrait exists — only his descendants' faces
narrative_blocks.body: There is no known portrait of Thomas Hooker. The statue that stands before the Old State House in Hartford — near the site of the First Meeting House — was sculpted by comparing the likenesses of his descendants.

narrative_blocks.header: Died 26 days after her last son born
narrative_blocks.body: John Shepard (her third child) was born April 2, 1646. Joanna died April 28, 1646 — almost certainly of childbed fever. She was roughly 24 years old. Her husband Thomas Shepard, who had already lost his first wife Margaret to illness weeks after arrival in New England, buried his second wife three years before his own death.

narrative_blocks.header: Sailed to New England with Thomas Hooker, 1633
narrative_blocks.body: She was born in England around 1621 and crossed the Atlantic with her father on the Griffin, arriving in New England in 1633 at roughly age 12. When Thomas Hooker led his congregation from Cambridge to found Hartford in 1636, Joanna remained behind — having married Thomas Shepard, the minister who succeeded her father's Cambridge pulpit.

narrative_blocks.header: Named after Mrs. Drake — her mother's employer
narrative_blocks.body: Her mother Susanna Garbrand was the woman-in-waiting to Mrs. Joan Drake of Esher, Surrey — the woman whose spiritual crisis under Thomas Hooker's pastoral care became the model for his entire theology of conversion. Thomas and Susanna married in 1621; Joanna was born that same year, almost certainly named for the woman at the center of her father's formative ministry.

narrative_blocks.header: Cared for her mother on the Hartford migration
narrative_blocks.body: According to the Newton family genealogy, Mary was the daughter responsible for caring for her mother Susanna on the arduous journey from Cambridge, Massachusetts to Hartford in 1636 — a journey in which Mrs. Hooker "was carried on a horse litter" in poor health. Mary was roughly fifteen years old and managed the Hooker household through her father's final years in Hartford until her marriage around 1644.

narrative_blocks.header: Stayed in Hartford while Newton built Farmington
narrative_blocks.body: When Roger Newton went to establish the church at Farmington in the late 1640s, Mary remained in Hartford — their son Samuel's baptism on October 20, 1646 appears on the Hartford register, placing her there while her husband laid the groundwork for the congregation he would formally found in 1652.

narrative_blocks.header: Eight children born across three Connecticut towns
narrative_blocks.body: The family's migrations are recorded in their children's birthplaces: Samuel and Roger Jr. were born in Hartford, Susannah and John in Farmington, Ezekiel back in Hartford, then Sarah, Mary, and Alice in Milford — where the family finally settled and where Mary died on February 4, 1676.

narrative_blocks.header: Father forbade it — he married and stayed
narrative_blocks.body: Thomas Hooker's will, written hours before his death in 1647, expressly addressed his son: "I doe not forbid my sonne John from seeking and taking a wife in England, yet I doe forbid him from marrying and tarrying there." John disobeyed on both counts — he married in England and never returned.

narrative_blocks.header: Jovial parson — dissipated son of Thomas Hooker
narrative_blocks.body: He was described by Edward Hooker as a "jovial, rollicking, sporting Parson" and "very dissipated." He chose Oxford University over his father's Emmanuel College Cambridge, was ordained by the Bishop of Oxford into the Church of England in 1660, and served as Vicar of Marsworth and then Rector of Leckhampstead in Buckinghamshire — a life in deliberate contrast to his father's Puritanism.

narrative_blocks.header: Found dead in bed — buried at midnight
narrative_blocks.body: He died in 1684, found dead in bed. He was buried privately at midnight — the manner of burial suggests the circumstances of his death were considered shameful or suspicious, possibly a self-inflicted death. His line ended with him; no verified descendants have been found.

narrative_blocks.header: The only Hooker line that ended in England
narrative_blocks.body: He was Thomas Hooker's first son and the eldest child of the second marriage, probably born about 1626 at Chelmsford, Essex. He came to New England with his father but returned to England for his education, finishing at Oxford after his father's death in 1647. His was the only Hooker line that did not continue in America.

burial.plot_notes: No stone survives. Well-attested tradition places the grave at the south side of Rev. Samuel Whitman's marked grave. "Mr. Whitman like Mr. Hooker, died while the minister of the town, and tradition says he was buried by the side of Mr. Hooker."

cross_connections.display_label: Five Hooker generations on the same Farmington ground.
Succeeded his brother-in-law as Farmington's minister.

narrative_blocks.header: The library — his, if his brother failed
narrative_blocks.body: Thomas Hooker's will gave his library to John on condition that John pursue ministry; if John faltered, the entire library and manuscripts went to Samuel. John did not become a minister. Samuel received his father's books — and then dedicated his own life to the ministry his father had wagered on him.

narrative_blocks.header: He persuaded Farmington to found a free school
narrative_blocks.body: In December 1685, the town of Farmington voted "to give £30 for a man to teach School for one year... and this School to be a free School for this town" — almost certainly at Hooker's instigation. It was among the earliest free public schools in colonial Connecticut, established a century and a half before public education became law.

narrative_blocks.header: Called to march — replaced two days later
narrative_blocks.body: On March 9, 1675-6, the Council ordered Hooker to prepare to march into King Philip's War with Major Treat's army toward Hadley and Northampton. Two days later, the Council quietly replaced him with Rev. Israel Chauncy. No reason was recorded — only the substitution.

narrative_blocks.header: He got each sermon into his heart first
narrative_blocks.body: Rev. Pitkin described him as "an excellent preacher, his composition good, his address pathetic, warm and engaging." Hooker told a friend he had "three things to do with his sermons before he delivered them in public: to write them, commit them unto his memory, and get them into his heart." His death was deplored as "a great breach upon this people."

ANECDOTE [cotton_mather_on_the_dead_hookers_living_son]: Cotton Mather wrote in his Magnalia Christi Americana: 'Thus we have to this day among us our dead Hooker, yet living in his worthy son Samuel Hooker, an able, faithful, useful minister at Farmington, in the Colony of Connecticut.' The phrase 'our dead Hooker yet living' captures both the weight of Thomas Hooker's legacy and Samuel's place beneath it — measured against his father for his entire mi
ANECDOTE [samuel_and_mary_brew_a_great_bowl_of_punch]: The wedding of Rev. Samuel Hooker and Mary Willet in 1658 was celebrated 'in a proper manner,' and an account handed down in the Willet family concludes the ceremony with: 'And Samuel and Mary did then brew a great bowl of punch.' The detail survived in family tradition for generations — one of the few personal moments recorded from Samuel Hooker's otherwise public-facing life.
ANECDOTE [no_stone_marks_his_grave]: Rev. Samuel Hooker died November 6, 1697 at Farmington and is buried in the Memento Mori Cemetery (CEM004), but no stone survives. Well-attested tradition points out the place as at the south side of the grave of his successor Rev. Samuel Whitman, which is appropriately marked. ANECDOTE [hartford_witch_trials_prosecutorial_tribunal]: EH ignores Samuel Hooker's role in the Hartford witch trials of the 1660s — which predate the Salem trials by nearly three decades. Connecticut was New England's most aggressive witch prosecutor: every person indicted in Connecticut's early years was convicted and hanged. Samuel Hooker of Farmington formed a prosecutorial tribunal with Rev. Samuel Stone of Hartford and Rev. Joseph Haynes of Wether
COMPILER CONNECTION: H00007 Samuel Hooker is the direct paternal ancestor of the compiler (Samuel Talcott Hooker). The Hooker surname line runs through Samuel's sons. Cotton Mather's phrase "our dead Hooker yet living in his worthy son" resonates across every generation of this line — including the current compiler, whose project this JSON is.

KING PHILIP'S WAR NOTE: March 9, 1675-6 — Council ordered Samuel to march with Major Treat's army. Two days later, March 11, replaced by Rev. Israel Chauncy (H.U. 1661) who went "as their chirurgion." Samuel did NOT march. No king_philips_war tag applied. Reason for replacement not recorded.

In May 1701, more than three years after his death, the Assembly ordered the last Election Sermon printed "upon the charge of the Colonie." Neither printing has been located.
HARVARD BILLS: Last quarter-bill dated 9 Dec 1654. Payments made by "Cap gookine," "mr hopkines," "Hezekiah Vsher," and others in "wheatte," "siluer," "porke," "butter," "rose watter." Charges include bringing wheat and pork from Boston. Chosen Fellow of the College November 27, 1654 — just before his last quarter-bill.
NICKNAME: "The Fervent Hooker" — on account of his earnestness and piety.

Thomas Shepard of Cambridge was one of Thomas Hooker's closest Puritan colleagues. Two sons of the two great Cambridge ministers, in the same Harvard class. narrative_blocks.header: Wrote his will — died three days later
narrative_blocks.body: He wrote his will on April 4, 1668; he died on April 7. He left his infant son Samuel — eight months old — to the care of "father and mother Flint" (Rev. Henry and Margery Flint of Braintree), with 100 acres at Rowley to come to the boy at age twenty-one. The will was proved April 22, 1668 (Essex County Probate #25186).

narrative_blocks.header: Both died within two months — probable epidemic
narrative_blocks.body: His wife Dorothy died February 12, 1668. He died April 7, 1668 — fifty-five days later. Their son Samuel was eight months old. Two young ministers dying weeks apart in the same town strongly suggests an epidemic moving through Rowley, though no specific disease is recorded in the sources.

narrative_blocks.header: Harvard at 17 — Rowley's minister at 24
narrative_blocks.body: He received his B.A. and A.M. from Harvard in 1658 at roughly age seventeen, then was ordained pastor of First Church Rowley on November 15, 1665 — the third minister of that congregation. He served for less than three years before his death at twenty-six.

narrative_blocks.header: Matthew Day's will called him "little Samuel"
narrative_blocks.body: Matthew Day — the Cambridge printer and Harvard figure — left him "the 3rd [silver spoon] that hath my own name on it which I brought out of England" in his will of May 10, 1649, addressing him as "my old acquaintance little Samuel Shepard." Samuel was seven years old; his mother had died three years earlier, his father just weeks before.

quotes.text: A very Precious, Holy, Meditating, Able and Choice Young Man. His Attainments in Communion with God, and in Daily Meditation and Close Walking, may shame those that are Elder than he. He was but Twenty six years of Age in October last. He was an Excellent Preacher, most dearly Beloved at Rowly, and of all that knew him; but just settled among them. The People would have Plucked out their Eyes for him, to have saved his Life.

AGE AT DEATH: 26
DEATH CIRCUMSTANCES: Died 55 days after wife Dorothy; both died in 1668, likely epidemic
RAISED BY: Brought up in grandfather Rev. Thomas Hooker's household in Hartford; fitted for college by him. One of the very few people who knew Thomas Hooker personally.
ORDINATION: Congregational pastor, Nov 15, 1665 at Rowley. Ordained pastor at Rowley. Third minister of Rowley.
MINISTRY: Pastor at First Church, Rowley 1665-1668. Third minister at Rowley. After his death, Mr. Phillips was ordained pastor and continued until his own death April 26, 1696.
WILL: Dated April 4, 1668, proved April 22, 1668. Citation: Essex County Probate file #25186; Probate Records of Essex County, Vol. 2:127; Suffolk County Probate file #488. Provisions: Named child Samuel; Left care and education of Samuel to 'father and mother Flint' (Rev. Henry and Margery Flint of Braintree); Flints to improve his estate for Samuel's upbringing; Father's 100 acres at Rowley to come to Samuel at age 21.
ANECDOTE (double_death_1668): Dorothy died February 12, 1668; Samuel died April 7, 1668 — 55 days apart, with infant son born August 1667. Almost certainly an epidemic moving through Rowley. Father's will written just three days before his death.
CHILD NOTE: Samuel Shepard (ID H00038) — Both parents dead within 8 months of his birth; raised by Flint grandparents. Birth: Aug 19, 1667.
BURIAL: Rowley Burial Ground, Rowley, Essex County, MA. Has gravestone. Plot: 4 Row, East (Marble) South front.
EDUCATION DISCREPANCY: Edward Hooker gives 1655. Gravestone inscription and church ordination records both give 1658. 1658 preferred — 1655 is likely confusion with uncle Rev. Samuel Hooker's 1653 graduation.
SIBLEY'S SOURCE: Half-brother of Thomas Shepard II (H.U. 1653, by first wife) and Jeremiah Shepard (H.U. 1669, by third wife). College charges dated 9-4-54 through 5-7-58. Affix of "Socius" suggests Fellow/Tutor but not confirmed in College Records. Admitted to full communion Cambridge church July 19, 1663; dismissed Aug 13, 1665 to Rowley church. Cotton Mather: "was one, whose Heart was a Tent in which the Lord remarkably chose to Dwell." Jonathan Mitchell quote preserved in quotes array.

narrative_blocks.header: Lived three months — first child named John
narrative_blocks.body: Born March 9, 1643 in Cambridge; died July 14, 1643 — three months and five days old. His parents Thomas Shepard and Joanna Hooker named their next surviving son John as well (H00010, b. 1646), as was common when a child died young.

research_notes: b. Mar 9, 1643 Cambridge MA; d. Jul 14, 1643 Cambridge MA. Died at approximately 3 months. NOTE: Prior data had birth and death year as 1644 — corrected to 1643 per genealogical sources. First of two children named John; his brother was also named John (H00010).

narrative_blocks.header: Militia ensign in King Philip's War
narrative_blocks.body: When King Philip's War broke out in 1675, Samuel was appointed ensign in the Milford militia at roughly age 29. He served through the war and its recurring aftermath of Indian skirmishes, rising to captain of the Milford militia by 1698.

narrative_blocks.header: Represented Milford fifteen times in the General Court
narrative_blocks.body: He represented Milford in fifteen sessions of the Connecticut General Court between 1691 and 1703 — a span of sustained civic service that ran alongside his captaincy of the militia. His father Roger Newton Sr. had helped found the town's church; Samuel served its government.

narrative_blocks.header: Married by Connecticut's future governor in 1680
narrative_blocks.body: His marriage to Lydia Ford on April 14, 1680 in Milford was performed by Major Robert Treat, then Deputy Governor of Connecticut. Treat would become Governor in 1683 and remain in office until 1698 — the same year John Newton died.

narrative_blocks.header: Died months before his last child was born
narrative_blocks.body: John Newton died in May 1699 — his estate was inventoried that month. His son John Jr. was born March 7, 1700, roughly ten months later. The boy was posthumous. Lydia Ford was left a widow at approximately thirty-seven with five children, the last not yet born.

narrative_blocks.header: Died with two books — one on felicity
narrative_blocks.body: His 1734 estate inventory listed clothing, a bed, a few animals, a few tools, and two books — one titled "Ye Felicity of Man." There was no real estate; the inventory indicates he was living in someone else's household. No heirs and no administrator were named.

narrative_blocks.header: Physician — died unmarried at 74
narrative_blocks.body: He was a physician in Milford and died May 4, 1734, age about 74, having never married. His gravestone reads: "Here lyes the body of Mr Ezekiel Newton aged about 74 years Died May the 4th 1734." He was the sixth child of Rev. Roger Newton and Mary Hooker, granddaughter of Thomas Hooker.

narrative_blocks.header: The yellow coat — never used
narrative_blocks.body: Roger Newton Sr.'s 1683 will left Ezekiel "one yellow say-coat for a child" — anticipating a family that never came. His brother Roger Jr. received a silk cap the same way; Roger's son Fletcher was baptized in that cap eighteen months later. Ezekiel's coat went unused. He died at seventy-four, unmarried.

narrative_blocks.header: A regicide praised his preaching in 1682
narrative_blocks.body: In 1682 John Wilson preached in New Haven for over a year. Col. John Dixwell — one of the English regicides then living in hiding in New Haven — wrote to Rev. Increase Mather calling him "a veire hopefull yonge man of good abilities." Wilson returned to Medfield in 1684 and eventually abandoned the ministry.

narrative_blocks.header: Left Harvard — his father taught him everything
narrative_blocks.body: He left Harvard College soon after admission, but received a thorough private education from his father in divinity, medicine, and law. He settled in Braintree in 1692 and spent the rest of his life farming, practicing medicine, and performing legal services.

narrative_blocks.body: His mother was Sarah Hooker Wilson (Thomas Hooker's daughter); his wife Sarah Newton's mother was Mary Hooker Newton (also Thomas Hooker's daughter). The two mothers were sisters. EH and the Wilson genealogy both explicitly note the cousin relationship.

burial.inscription: Here lies Inter'd ye Remains of Mrs. Susanna Rawson Relict of ye late Rev'd Mr. Grindall Rawson, Pastor of a Church of Christ in Mendon & Daughter of ye Rev'd Mr. John Wilson 1st Minister of ye Gospel in Medfield who changed mortality for life Aug. 23, 1691. She was born Dec. 1, 1664, & died July 8, 1748, in ye 84th year of her age.

narrative_blocks.header: Wrote Cotton Mather a eulogy for her husband
narrative_blocks.body: After Rev. Grindal Rawson died on February 6, 1715, Susanna wrote a long letter to Cotton Mather — a detailed portrait of her husband's forty-year ministry at Mendon, his Indian mission work, his character, and his final hours. His last words were "Come Lord Jesus, come quickly!" The letter was published in the Rawson Family Memorial. Cotton Mather quoted it in the sermon he preached at Rawson's funeral.

narrative_blocks.header: Her gravestone names her father, not husband
narrative_blocks.body: Her gravestone in Mendon reads: "Here lies inter'd ye Remains of Mrs. Susanna Rawson, Relict of ye late Rev. Mr. Grindal Rawson... and daughter to ye Rev. Mr. John Wilson 1st Minister of ye Gospel in Medfield." She outlived her husband by 33 years, dying July 8, 1748, age 83.

sources.full: Gary Boyd Roberts, Ancestors of American Presidents

research_notes:
EASTER EGG: Susanna Wilson Rawson — ancestor of President William Howard Taft; rare surviving female letter-writer of period
TAGS REMOVED: literate_woman and ancestor_of_president (non-canonical). letter_writer tag retained.
b. Dec 1, 1664 Medfield. d. Jul 8, 1748 Mendon, Worcester Co. MA. Grindal and Susanna were second cousins: his paternal grandmother and her paternal grandfather (Rev. John Wilson Sr.) were siblings. One of the ancestors of President William Howard Taft (per FindAGrave/sources). Her letter to Cotton Mather about Rawson's death is one of the most detailed surviving letters written by a woman in 18th-century New England.
TAFT DOUBLE CONNECTION: Two men from the same Taft family each married a Hooker/Rawson descendant: (1) Moses Taft (I00112) married H00218 Mary Dorr Taft, granddaughter of Susanna. (2) Aaron Taft (I00117) married HD0339 Rhoda Rawson Taft, great-granddaughter of Susanna. Moses and Aaron's father were brothers (sons of Capt. Joseph Taft b.1680). William Howard Taft (gen 8) descends through Aaron's line, not Moses's.
ANCESTOR OF NOTABLE: William Howard Taft — 27th President of the United States. Source: Roberts 2009
WRITINGS: Letter to Cotton Mather ca.after 1715 — Rare surviving female voice from period. Source: Rawson Family Memorial 1875

narrative_blocks.header: His wife's grandfather wrote a book Boston burned
narrative_blocks.body: Mary's mother was Ann Pynchon, daughter of William Pynchon — who founded Springfield, Massachusetts, and whose 1650 book "The Meritorious Price of Our Redemption" became the first book publicly burned in New England. Thomas Hooker — grandson of Hartford's founder — married into Springfield's founding family.

career.notes: Physician in Hartford. Also administered estate of Richard Lord II (wife's first husband) from 1686 until released Feb 8, 1693.

research_notes:
TITLE NOTE: EH gives no title. Multiple secondary sources call him "Dr. Thomas Hooker" and "physician in Hartford." One online genealogy source labels him "Rev." — this appears to be an error confusing him with his grandfather Rev. Thomas Hooker. Use "Dr." pending further evidence.
DEATH NOTE: Most sources say d. 1720 Hartford, age 61. One source gives "Oct 13, 1756" — clearly wrong (would make him 97). 1720 confirmed by EH.
BIRTH LOCATION: EH says "b. Plymouth, Mass." Some sources say Farmington. His parents were in Plymouth until 1661 and Thomas was born June 10, 1659 — Plymouth is correct.
MARRIAGE: Between Feb 20, 1686 and Oct 21, 1686 per genealogical records. Mary Smith was widow of Richard Lord II (X02135), who was lost at sea Nov 5, 1685.
PYNCHON CONNECTION: Mary's mother Ann Pynchon was daughter of Y00004 Hon. William Pynchon, founder of Springfield and author of the first book burned in New England (1650). No entry yet for Ann Pynchon Smith — flag for future session.
CHILDREN: None with Mary Smith. No issue.

narrative_blocks.header: His house stood south of the burial ground
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel Hooker II had a fine residence at Farmington, next south of the old burial ground — later known as the Mix house — and ran a store for mercantile business in Hartford. He was one of the first of the Farmington Hookers to move from ministry to commerce.

education.notes: Confirmed by Julius Gay, Historical Pamphlets (Farmington 1890-1911): "fresh from the honors of Harvard." Also noted as "fellow of that college."

career.notes: Mercantile business. Store and fine residence at Farmington.

sources.notes: Samuel Hooker Jr. biography; confirms Plymouth birth, Hartford merchant activity

narrative_blocks.header: Store at Farmington's most prominent corner
narrative_blocks.body: William Hooker's store stood at the most prominent corner in Farmington — opposite his father's house, at the junction of main street and the road over the stone bridge. He died at 26, leaving only a daughter, Susannah, who was named in her grandfather Rev. Samuel Hooker's will.

career.notes: Store at the most prominent corner in Farmington, opposite his father's house, at the junction of main street and the road over the stone bridge.

research_notes:
Died age 26. Will dated same year as death.

OCCUPATION NOTES: Merchant at Farmington; store opposite his father Rev. Samuel Hooker's house, on the corner of the main street and the road over the stone bridge over the Pequabuc River — the place known as Thomas Cowles corner.
AGE AT DEATH: 26
DEATH DISCREPANCY: Some sources give death 1716. EH says 1689. Will dated Aug 8, 1689 with inventory taken same year. Father's 1697 will refers to "William Hooker Deceased." 1689 confirmed.

burial.notes: Memento Mori Cemetery, Farmington, Hartford County, Connecticut. Same cemetery as his father Rev. Samuel Hooker (H00007).

cross_connections.notes: Col. Wadsworth's son Daniel Wadsworth founded the Wadsworth Atheneum in Hartford — a monument with Hooker family kinship interest. Research needed to confirm Mehitable Russell's Hooker descent line. Warren 1887 confirms: Elizabeth Standley married John Wadsworth — their son Daniel Wadsworth founded the Wadsworth Atheneum.

cross_connections.notes: Capt. Thomas Hart b. ca. 1644, d. Aug 27, 1726 age 82. Son of Stephen Hart (X00034). Brother of John Hart who survived the 1666 fire (X00033). Not in this tree through marriage — noted as civic context only.
cross_connections.display_label: Both helped govern Farmington for decades.
Wadsworth's wife descended from both Hooker and Standley.
Both served the public life of Hartford's founding families.
His wife's sister — grandmother of Col. Jeremiah Wadsworth.
Four of their children married children of Capt. Hart.

narrative_blocks.header: Dark hair, dark eyes — dignity commanding respect
narrative_blocks.body: His grandson described him: "Dark hair and dark eyes, and a dignity in his air and expression that procured profound respect." A fine looking man, tall, very erect, and had a prominent large nose. He lived his entire life in Farmington, dying under the shade of the elm his father had planted for him on his wedding day.

narrative_blocks.header: He defended Tunxis land rights in court
narrative_blocks.body: In 1738, John Hooker provided a formal statement to the Connecticut General Court that ninety acres at Indian Neck had "always belonged" to the Tunxis tribe — an unusual act of testimony on behalf of the indigenous people whose land his own family's town had displaced.

narrative_blocks.header: The bell tolled — Hooker stepped out first
narrative_blocks.body: A chronicler recorded that every Sunday the bell rang, then began tolling; at the first stroke, Hooker issued from his house and proceeded to the Meeting House. The minister walked from his house. Whoever arrived first waited at the door; together they entered, the congregation rose, and they walked the aisle side by side — Hooker to his pew, the minister to the pulpit, both sitting down at the same moment.

narrative_blocks.header: The elm fell so someone could walk straight
narrative_blocks.body: His father gave him a site on the west side of Farmington's main street, where he built under the branches of a great elm. The tree became famous through all the region for the company that gathered beneath it. Three generations of Hookers kept the house and the elm. When the last Hooker died in 1830, new owners laid an axe to its roots because it interfered with a straight path to the street.

career.notes: Served as justice and register of Farmington
From Farmington. Served as Clerk two sessions; Speaker six sessions.
Served as judge of Superior Court concurrent with assistant role
Commissioner of the Mohegan's sequestered land at New London

sources.full: L.J. Hooker, 'Edward Hooker, Commander, U.S. Navy,' The Old Northwest Genealogical Quarterly (Columbus, Ohio, 1898-), VII, 12

EH spells wife's surname 'Stanley' in his entry header; entries 99-101 spell it 'Standley'. 'Standley' is the correct original spelling per EH narrative — early Hartford settlers spelled it with a 'd'. The name has since become standardized as 'Stanley' in America. See also LM002 Stanley-Whitman House, Farmington.

Served as witness, certifying Assistant, and Registrar in Sue Squa deed to William Hooker (H00089), Jun 13, 1733 (DOC004) — three official roles in a single land transaction.

cross_connections.display_label: His wife's grandfather governed the Connecticut Colony.

narrative_blocks.header: He married the granddaughter of Governor William Leete
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Leete was the daughter of William Leete Jr. and the granddaughter of Governor William Leete — the only man to govern both New Haven Colony and the Colony of Connecticut. James Hooker, born a Farmington pastor's son, married into one of the most distinguished families in colonial Connecticut.

narrative_blocks.header: First Probate Judge — presided over the Assembly
narrative_blocks.body: He became the first Probate Judge of the Guilford district and repeatedly represented Guilford in the Colonial Assembly, serving for a number of sessions as its presiding officer. EH notes he "became a very prominent man at Guilford."

narrative_blocks.header: His only son died at 21, name extinct
narrative_blocks.body: EH notes he "had but one son who died unmarried, leaving no one to perpetuate his name." That son was William (H00108), Yale class of 1722, who died at 21.

career.notes: First Probate Judge of the Guilford district.
Repeatedly represented Guilford. Presiding officer for several sessions.

research_notes:
OCCUPATION NOTES: Became a very prominent man at Guilford. First Probate Judge of the Guilford district. Repeatedly represented Guilford in the Colonial Assembly and was for a number of sessions its presiding officer.
DEATH DATE DISCREPANCY: EH gives "Mar. 12, 1740 or 1741." 1740 preferred.

narrative_blocks.header: Betrothed at 29 — passed before the wedding
narrative_blocks.body: Roger Hooker was betrothed to Mary Standley of Hartford when he died on April 29, 1698, at age 29. Seven months later — December 28, 1698 — his brother Nathaniel married her instead.

research_notes:
Betrothed to Mary Stanley; she married his brother Nathaniel after Roger's death.
NOTE: betrothal CC to I00051 removed — not a valid CC type. Story in NB1.
NOTE: regicide CC to X00067 Gov. Leete removed — Roger was born 1668; regicide sheltering was 1661-1668. Wrong person. This story may belong on H00007 or H00001.
REGICIDE NETWORK: H00032 appears in notable_story "regicide_network" (person_ids: I00003, X00067, H00032). His grandfather Thomas Hooker's family network was deeply involved in sheltering regicides Whalley and Goffe. Roger was born 1668 so could not have personally sheltered them — but the family connection may explain his inclusion. Possibly should be H00007 (his father Samuel Hooker) instead. See also: H00024 John Wilson (first cousin once removed) who corresponded with regicide John Dixwell in New Haven. The Hooker-Wilson-Newton network is the core regicide network in Connecticut.
DEATH LOCATION: EH gives none. Removed assumption of Farmington.

narrative_blocks.header: His bride had been promised to his brother
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Standley had been betrothed to Nathaniel's brother Roger Hooker, who died April 29, 1698. Seven months later — December 28, 1698 — Nathaniel married her instead.

narrative_blocks.header: His wedding gift was half of Main Street
narrative_blocks.body: When Nathaniel married, his father-in-law Hon. Nathaniel Stanley gave him half his Hartford lot — extending from the Second Burial Ground to the little river. This became his place of business on the main street, just below the present Centre Church.

narrative_blocks.header: Two different Stanley families both married Hooker brothers
narrative_blocks.body: His brother John had married Abigail Standley of one Stanley family; Nathaniel married Mary Standley of a completely separate Stanley branch — Hon. Nathaniel Stanley of Hartford's line. Two unrelated families sharing the same name both converged on the Hooker line in the same generation.

narrative_blocks.header: Hartford's Colonial Assembly representative — dead at 40
narrative_blocks.body: He represented Hartford in the Connecticut Colonial Assembly for several years. He died November 21, 1711, at age 40, still in office.

research_notes:
Son of Rev. Samuel Hooker (H00007) and Mary Willet (I00006). Brother of Hon. John Hooker (H00030) who married Abigail Standley. m. Mary Standley/Stanley (I00051), daughter of Hon. Nathaniel Stanley of Hartford. Stanley book entry 10 (MARY STANLEY): "married Nathaniel Hooker, son of Samuel and Mary (Willett) Hooker." Children (Stanley book): Mary (b. Dec 3, 1699, d. unm. Jan 2, 1765), Alice (b. Nov 12, 1701, m. D. Howard, d. 1750), Sarah (b. Nov 7, 1704, m. Hon. Daniel Edwards of Hartford), Abigail (b. 1707, m. Rev. Benjamin Lord DD of Norwich), Nathaniel (b.?, m. Eunice Talcott, Yale 1729 = H00115). His wife's father Hon. Nathaniel Stanley was of Farmington. NOTE: This is a DIFFERENT Stanley family from Capt. John Stanley (X00035, father of Abigail who married H00030 John Hooker). Two separate Stanley branches both intersecting the Hooker line.

OCCUPATION NOTES: Noted merchant and prominent man at Hartford. Place of business on main street a little below present Centre Church, on property originally the building lot of his father-in-law Nathaniel Standley, who gave half his lot (extending from Second Burial Ground to the little river) to Nathaniel Hooker at marriage.
TALCOTT-EDWARDS CONVERGENCE (queued for research): His daughter Sarah Hooker (H00112) married Hon. Daniel Edwards (I00053, b.Apr 11, 1701) — whose mother was Mary Talcott Edwards (T00042), daughter of Lt.-Col. John Talcott (T00035). Daniel was also a half-brother of Richard Edwards, father of Jonathan Edwards the theologian. Their children are therefore both Hooker AND Talcott descendants. The Jonathan Edwards connection also runs through this line. See I00053 and T00042 for further research.
DISPLAY NAME NOTE: "Willett" added as middle name per Sam. Sam wrote "Rev." but EH describes him as a noted merchant — not a minister. Title left null. Verify if ministerial ordination documented.

name.photo_notes: Portrait of Mrs. James Pierpont (née Mary Hooker), 1711. Wikipedia Commons.

narrative_blocks.header: Granddaughter of Thomas Hooker — married Yale's co-founder
narrative_blocks.body: She was the daughter of Rev. Samuel Hooker and granddaughter of Rev. Thomas Hooker, co-founder of Hartford. In 1698 she married Rev. James Pierpont Sr. — the minister who secured the Yale charter in 1701. Her portrait (ART040) was painted by the Pierpont Limner in 1711, now at Yale Art Gallery (companion to her husband's portrait).

narrative_blocks.header: Her daughter Sarah married Jonathan Edwards
narrative_blocks.body: Her daughter Sarah Pierpont (HD0418) married the theologian Jonathan Edwards in 1727. Their descendants became one of the most studied family trees in American history, tracing influence through generations of ministers, educators, and public figures.

narrative_blocks.header: His first wife died of cold at church
narrative_blocks.body: Before Mary, James Pierpont had married twice. His first wife Abigail Davenport died February 3, 1692 — she had followed the custom of attending church in her wedding suit on the Sunday after the ceremony and caught cold. She was dead within weeks. Mary was his third wife, and survived him by 26 years.

research_notes:
b. ca. 1673; d. ca. 1727. Daughter of Samuel Hooker (H00007) and [mother I00006] — granddaughter of Rev. Thomas Hooker. Portrait survives (one of the few colonial-era portraits of a woman in this line). MARRIED REV. JAMES PIERPONT SR. (X00126): Together, they are the parental foundation of the entire Pierpont branch of the tree. Rev. James Pierpont Sr. was a co-founder of Yale College in 1701. His wife Mary Hooker was the granddaughter of the founder of Hartford. Their children include: James Pierpont II (H00116), Mary Pierpont Russell (H00118), Sarah Pierpont Edwards (HD0418) — wife of Jonathan Edwards — and others. Through Sarah Pierpont Edwards: the line leads to Aaron Burr, 3rd VP. Through James Pierpont II: the line leads to J.P. Morgan, Jingle Bells composer, Vermont Chief Justice, Yale mathematician, Titanic victim, Medal of Honor. Through Mary Pierpont Russell: the line leads to the Russell-Talcott convergence.

EASTER EGG: Mary Hooker Pierpont — married Rev. James Pierpont, co-founder of Yale College 1701; portrait survives
CHILDREN AUDIT NOTE: Marriage m1 has 8 children (H00116, HD0414, H00118, HD0416, HD1322, HD0417, HD0418, HD0419). EH lists 7 (H00116-H00122). H00117 (Samuel, drowned) and H00119-H00122 are EH entries not in the marriage list — they may have duplicate HD-prefix entries. HD1322 and HD0417 are both named Benjamin Pierpont — likely duplicates. Review needed: are H00117/H00119-H00122 AND HD0414/HD0416/HD1322/HD0417/HD0418/HD0419 the same people under different IDs? Do not merge without careful verification.

cross_connections.display_label: He tutored under Pierson — Yale's first rector.

narrative_blocks.header: First Yale tutor and first Yale B.A. graduate
narrative_blocks.body: He was the first tutor appointed at the Collegiate School (Yale), hired by first rector Abraham Pierson Jr. (X02127). He was also the first student to receive a Yale B.A. degree in 1703, before leaving to practice medicine. As a grandson of Rev. Thomas Hooker, he was the direct connection between Hartford's founding family and New England's new seat of learning.

narrative_blocks.header: Surgeon of the Canada expedition, 1711
narrative_blocks.body: Dr. Daniel Hooker served as surgeon of the Massachusetts expedition against Canada in 1711 — the final major military operation of Queen Anne's War. He then returned to Wethersfield to continue practicing medicine and law.

narrative_blocks.header: Declined his father's pulpit — chose medicine
narrative_blocks.body: After Rev. Samuel Hooker died in 1697, Farmington invited Daniel to succeed his father. He declined. He chose medicine instead, studying law alongside it — not to practice, but so he could help patients make their wills.

education.notes: B.A. Harvard 1700. First Farmington-born man to receive a college education.

Received degree at Yale's first commencement 1703. EH says he was "the first B.A. of Yale" but also that Rev. John Hart (I00137) was "nominally the second, but really the first actual student to graduate." Daniel was the tutor/faculty; Hart was the first student B.A. Conflict flagged — see research_notes.

career.notes: First tutor of Yale Collegiate School. Hired by rector Abraham Pierson Jr. (X02127). Resigned in favor of Rev. John Hart (I00137).
Settled Wethersfield after leaving Yale. Also studied law to help patients make wills — no evidence he practiced law professionally.

sources.full: Israel P. Warren D.D., The Stanley Families of America (1887)

Do not assert Daniel as "first B.A." without this nuance.

burial.plot_notes: Near East Norwalk railway station — old burying ground. Her husband and her mother (I00006 Mary Willet) are buried near each other here. "It is probable that her grave is near these other two, but no stone marks the place." (EH)

narrative_blocks.header: The most accomplished lady Norwalk had ever seen
narrative_blocks.body: EH recorded that Sarah Hooker "was accounted the most accomplished lady that had ever come to Norwalk." The story of her stately grace as "Dame Buckingham" was still preserved in the annals of the town when EH wrote in 1909 — nearly 230 years after her marriage.

narrative_blocks.header: No stone marks her grave — tradition knows
narrative_blocks.body: Her husband Rev. Stephen Buckingham and her mother Mary Willet are buried near each other in the old burying ground at East Norwalk. EH notes that her own grave is "probably near these other two, but no stone marks the place."

Stephen Buckingham (X01015). So her stepfather's son became her husband. After her stepfather died (1709), her mother came to live with Sarah and Stephen at Norwalk until Mary's death in 1712.

narrative_blocks.header: They met in the same household in Surrey
narrative_blocks.body: Susanna Garbrand was working as a waiting-gentlewoman to Mrs. Joanna Drake at Esher, Surrey, when Thomas Hooker arrived in 1620 as curate of Esher church — "having his diet and lodging" in the Drake household. They married at Amersham on April 3, 1621, the ceremony performed by Susanna's own uncle by marriage, Rev. Robert Chaloner.

narrative_blocks.header: Mrs. Drake left thirty pounds to Joanna Hooker
narrative_blocks.body: The will of Francis Drake, dated March 13, 1633/4, gave "Joanna Hooker, now in New England, 30 pounds at her day of marriage" — Joanna was the eldest child of Thomas and Susanna, and goddaughter of Mrs. Drake, the woman in whose household her parents had met.

narrative_blocks.header: She crossed the wilderness on a horse litter
narrative_blocks.body: During the 1636 migration from Cambridge to Hartford — a hundred miles through wilderness with no path, guided only by compass — Susanna Hooker was carried on a horse litter, having been ill. The company of one hundred drove 160 head of cattle before them, felled trees for bridges, and made the journey in a fortnight.

narrative_blocks.header: Mrs. Drake was dying — Hooker brought peace
narrative_blocks.body: Thomas Hooker had been brought to Esher specifically to counsel Mrs. Drake, who was convinced she was damned and unredeemable. His counsel brought her to an ecstatic conversion shortly before her death in 1625 — and in the household where all this unfolded, he met and fell in love with Susanna Garbrand.

research_notes:
DEATH SOURCE NOTE: Probably as second wife of Elder William Goodwin
ORIGIN: Born ca.1593 in Oxford (Garbrand family). After mother died 1609, lived with childless aunt Mrs. Christian (Garbrand) Chaloner, wife of Rev. Robert Chaloner D.D., rector of Amersham, Buckinghamshire. Became "waiting-gentlewoman" to Mrs. Joanna (Tothill) Drake at Esher, Surrey.
MIGRATION NOTE: Sam mentions Susanna may have been carried in a litter or conveyance during the 1636 wilderness migration from Cambridge to Hartford. Source needed — when confirmed, add as NB4.
WILLIAM GOODWIN NOTE: EH 1909 and Flagg 1926 suggest Susanna became second wife of Elder William Goodwin after Thomas Hooker's death (1647). Goodwin was named overseer of Thomas Hooker's will alongside Governor Edward Hopkins. Walter Gray slander charge (Connecticut Colony Records): Gray punished for "his gross abuse & evil in slandering Mr. Will[iam] Goodwyn & Mar. Hooker in charging them with the act of adultery."
SLANDER CASE (not in NBs — too sensitive for public project): Walter Gray was brought before the Connecticut court and punished for "his gross abuse & evil in slandering Mr. Will[iam] Goodwyn & Mar. Hooker in charging them with the act of adultery." This occurred around the time of or before Susanna's second marriage to Goodwin. Source: Connecticut Colony Records.

parents.notes: Son of William Shepard of Towcester, England

cross_connections.display_label: Both prosecuted Hutchinson — Shepard's voice, Wilson's verdict.
Both moderated the Hutchinson trial, 1637.

narrative_blocks.header: Harvard came to Cambridge because of his ministry
narrative_blocks.body: A plaque at Harvard University, quoting Cotton Mather, records that it was "in consideration of the salutary effect of Shepard's ministry" that Harvard College came to be placed at Newtowne (now Cambridge). Harvard was founded in 1636 — the same year Shepard settled there after Hooker's congregation removed to Hartford.

narrative_blocks.header: Took Hooker's pulpit — and bought Hooker's house
narrative_blocks.body: When Thomas Hooker led his congregation from Newtowne to Hartford in 1636, Shepard took over as minister of First Church Cambridge and purchased Hooker's Newtowne property. He was the third minister at the church — succeeding the man whose daughter he would later marry.

narrative_blocks.header: Anne Hutchinson's most vocal clerical critic at trial
narrative_blocks.body: During the Antinomian Controversy (1637-1638), Shepard sat with the colonial ministers at both the civil and church trials of Anne Hutchinson. He was among her most vocal critics during the church trial, which ended in her excommunication. The controversy turned on whether salvation required evidence of sanctification — a question central to Shepard's own pastoral method.

narrative_blocks.header: Margaret died weeks after landing — of consumption
narrative_blocks.body: His first wife Margaret Touteville (X00999) sailed with him and their infant son on the Defence, arriving at Boston on October 3, 1635. She died of consumption at Cambridge in February 1636 — weeks after landing. Shepard recorded her death and his grief in his autobiography. He married Joanna Hooker (H00002), Thomas Hooker's daughter, in 1640.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at 43 of a peritonsillar abscess
narrative_blocks.body: He died at Cambridge on August 25, 1649, age 43, of quinsy — a peritonsillar abscess, a complication of tonsillitis. He had survived the deaths of two wives and several children. He left an autobiography, published posthumously, that remains one of the most personal records of Puritan spiritual life in colonial New England.

parents.notes: Son of Samuel Newton, said to be of same family as Sir Isaac Newton, whose tomb is in Westminster Abbey. The name Isaac has been continued in some branches of the Newton family in every generation.

Both sheltered the regicides — Newton in Milford, Leete in Guilford.
Trained him in theology — he became Yale's first rector.
His brother-in-law Samuel succeeded him at Farmington.

narrative_blocks.header: Hart family burned alive — Newton fled Farmington
narrative_blocks.body: In 1657 tensions at Farmington came to a head: a man named Scott was murdered, and the house of John Hart caught fire — Hart and most of his family perished in the flames, though one son survived. Newton left the Farmington church that year; his brother-in-law Samuel Hooker eventually succeeded him as pastor.

narrative_blocks.header: The captain called him Jonah — and left
narrative_blocks.body: In October 1657, waiting in Boston for a ship to England, Newton received a letter inviting him to town for a special service — and left the ship while it was detained by headwinds. The captain, per John Hull's diary, concluded Newton was the Jonah "trying to escape from his duty" and causing the contrary winds — and sailed without him. Newton went to England the following spring.

narrative_blocks.header: First pastor of Farmington — seven pillars church
narrative_blocks.body: First pastor of Farmington, Connecticut — one of the Seven Pillars of its founding church (1652)

narrative_blocks.header: Tunxis missionary — trained by Thomas Hooker
narrative_blocks.body: Did missionary work among the Tunxis Indians; among first Connecticut ministers to admit Native Americans to full church membership with voting rights on town affairs

narrative_blocks.header: Witnessed the 1650 Connecticut-Tunxis land agreement
narrative_blocks.body: Witness to 1650 agreement between Colony of Connecticut and Tunxis Indians

narrative_blocks.header: Yale's first rector learned theology at Newton's table
narrative_blocks.body: Trained Abraham Pierson in his home at Milford — Pierson became the first President of Yale College

narrative_blocks.header: Married Hooker's daughter — studied under Hooker himself
narrative_blocks.body: Son-in-law and student of Rev. Thomas Hooker; married Hooker's daughter Mary

narrative_blocks.header: Possibly related to Sir Isaac Newton
narrative_blocks.body: Said to be of same family as Sir Isaac Newton

narrative_blocks.header: Largest library in New Haven Colony at death
narrative_blocks.body: The inventory of Newton's estate in June 1683 included Greek and Latin volumes, biblical commentaries, travel accounts, and domestic remedies — appraised at £50 11s 7d in books alone. It was the largest library inventoried in the New Haven Colony at that time.

education.notes: Studied at Harvard but name not on graduate list. Some early Harvard records in which his name would naturally appear were accidentally burned; uncertain he received a diploma. Cotton Mather nevertheless speaks of him as one of the young students who came from England to finish their education in America.

parents.father_research_notes: Son of Rev. John Wilson Sr., first minister First Church Boston

narrative_blocks.header: Founded Medfield church — served forty years
narrative_blocks.body: He became the first minister of Medfield, Massachusetts in 1651 and served the congregation for forty years until his death in 1691. He had previously served as a colleague of Rev. Richard Mather at Dorchester, and received his education at Harvard College in 1642.

narrative_blocks.header: Harvard 1642 — the first graduating class
narrative_blocks.body: He attended Harvard College in 1642, effectively the first graduating class of the institution. His father Rev. John Wilson Sr. was a close ally of Rev. Thomas Hooker, and John Jr. married Hooker's daughter Sarah — bringing together two of the founding families of Puritan New England.

narrative_blocks.header: Son of Boston's founding minister
narrative_blocks.body: His father, Rev. John Wilson Sr., was the first minister of the First Church of Boston from its founding in 1630 until his death in 1667 — the man who formally excommunicated Anne Hutchinson in 1638 and who supervised the execution of Mary Dyer in 1660.

narrative_blocks.header: Fell four stories — taken up for dead
narrative_blocks.body: Cotton Mather records that as a child, Wilson "fell upon his head from a loft four stories high into the street; from whence he was taken up for dead, and so battered and bruised and bloody with his fall, that it struck horror into the beholders." His father's prayers were credited with his recovery. He went on to serve Medfield forty years as minister, physician, and schoolmaster.

career.notes: Studied at Eaton's school (introductory to Harvard). Testified to ill-treatment of scholars by Eaton.
Colleague of Rev. Richard Mather at Dorchester. Known as "the gracious and godly Mr. Wilson."
Settled 1651. Ordained pastor Oct 12, 1652. Also served as physician and schoolmaster. Served 40 years until his death.

narrative_blocks.header: Named for Elizabeth I's Archbishop of Canterbury
narrative_blocks.body: He was named "Grindal" after Edmund Grindal, Archbishop of Canterbury under Elizabeth I — his great-great-grandmother's brother, per the Harvard commencement of 1678. At that ceremony President Urian Oakes called him "sprung from a most illustrious stock" and prayed that Rawson might "in learning, holiness and excellence of character, resemble both Wilson and Grindal."

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter of New York City's first mayor
narrative_blocks.body: Her father Capt. Thomas Willet was appointed the first Mayor of New York City in 1665, when the English renamed the Dutch colony. He had previously succeeded Capt. Miles Standish as commander of Plymouth's militia — one of the most storied military commands in early New England. Mary carried this distinguished lineage to Farmington when she married Rev. Samuel Hooker.

narrative_blocks.header: Three generations of Willet women known for beauty
narrative_blocks.body: Mary's mother was described as possessing 'beauty and majesty almost divine.' A portrait of Mary's daughter Mary Hooker Pierpont survives, and from it contemporaries formed 'the opinion that she was a woman of uncommon beauty.' Her daughter Sarah was 'accounted the most accomplished lady that had ever come to Norwalk.'

narrative_blocks.header: Remarried at 66 — outlived two minister husbands
narrative_blocks.body: After Samuel Hooker's death in 1697, Mary married Reverend Thomas Buckingham of Saybrook at age 66. When he died in 1709 she moved to Norwalk to live with her son-in-law Reverend Stephen Buckingham, where she remained until her death in 1712 at age 74.

research_notes:
Mary Willet — daughter of first Mayor of New York City; Willet family prominent Plymouth and New York colonists

NOTE FOR MARTHA WILLET ENTRY: Mary's sister Martha Willet was the first wife of Judge Saffin. After Martha's death, Saffin married twice more, but left a record written after his third marriage that on the anniversary of Martha's death it was his custom 'to seclude himself and give way to his inconsolable grief for the irreparable loss he had sustained in the death of his beloved Martha.' The record of a man annually mourning his first wife across two subsequent marriages is one of the more striking personal documents from colonial New England.
MARRIAGE NOTE (m2): Married at age 66
M2 NOTE: After Samuel Hooker died Nov 6, 1697, Mary (then 65) married Rev. Thomas Buckingham of Saybrook CT on Aug 10, 1703 (aged 65). He died April 1, 1709. Mary then went to live with her son-in-law Rev. Stephen Buckingham at Norwalk — who was the SON of her second husband Thomas Buckingham, AND the husband of her own daughter Sarah Hooker (H00037). She died at Norwalk June 24, 1712. Her grave is in the old burying ground near East Norwalk railway station.

narrative_blocks.header: Her mother was sister to Harvard's president
narrative_blocks.body: Her mother Margery Flint was the eldest sister of Leonard Hoar, who served as President of Harvard from 1672 to 1675. Dorothy died February 12, 1668 at Rowley, age twenty-five — fifty-five days before her husband Samuel Shepard, leaving their eight-month-old son Samuel to be raised by her parents.

narrative_blocks.body: He preached in Dunstable from May 1, 1679, gathered a church, and was ordained December 16, 1685. A 1702 diary entry records: "Mr. Thomas Weld, the pastor of the Church of Dunstable was buryed — he was an eminent preacher of the word of god, a man well beloued and much Lamented by them that knew him." narrative_blocks.body: From 1691, Indian hostilities during King William's War (1689-1697) so alarmed his Dunstable congregation that "near two thirds of the inhabitants moved away." Weld preached through years of frontier danger, died June 7, 1702, at the very beginning of Queen Anne's War — Dunstable was among the most exposed settlements in Massachusetts.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale's first Bachelor's degree — a Harvard sophomore who rode down to salvage a Connecticut experiment
narrative_blocks.summary: John Hart spent two years at Harvard before transferring to the struggling Collegiate School of Connecticut in 1702. On September 15, 1703, he graduated alone at Saybrook — Yale's first and only bachelor's degree recipient that year. His original diploma is preserved in the Yale Library.
narrative_blocks.detail: Hart's path to the degree was unconventional. When Rector Pierson needed a tutor, Hart — still working toward his own B.A. — served without pay, collecting only student fines for disobedience. This arrangement triggered Yale's first student rebellion, promptly squelched by the Trustees who thanked Hart and awarded him £50 for his trouble. He was, in the same moment, Yale's first bursary recipient and its first disciplinary crisis. 'Young John Hart rode down from Cambridge,' writes Oviatt, 'and became the first actual student in the College advanced to the honor of a Bachelor's Degree.' (Yale Biographical Sketches 1701-1745; Oviatt, Beginnings of Yale; yale60.org)

narrative_blocks.header: Founding minister of East Guilford (now Madison) — 'one of the first eminence of preachers in his day'
narrative_blocks.summary: Ordained November 25, 1707, Hart served as the founding minister of East Guilford for 24 years until his death in 1731. The town granted him 25 acres as settlement. President Stiles later said he was 'a man of the greatest ingenuity and learning of all the seven' ministers he knew.
narrative_blocks.detail: Hart had preceded Daniel Hooker (son of Rev. Samuel Hooker of Farmington, H00007) as Yale's second tutor, then entered the ministry in Connecticut. About 80 members were added to his church during his pastorate. His third wife was Mary Hooker (H00105), granddaughter of Thomas Hooker and daughter of Judge James Hooker of Guilford — connecting Yale's first graduate to Hartford's founding family. His estate at death was appraised at approximately £1,900, including books valued at £35 — substantial for a colonial minister. (Yale Biographical Sketches 1701-1745; EH 1909)

education.notes: FIRST student at Yale to receive a Bachelor's degree — graduated alone at Saybrook, September 15, 1703. His original diploma is preserved in Yale Library. The 1702 class received M.A. degrees only (honorary, for Harvard graduates); Hart's 1703 B.A. was Yale's first bachelor's degree.

narrative_blocks.header: Sea captain, judge, colonel — a full Connecticut life
narrative_blocks.body: Matthew Talcott was a sea captain, merchant, and ship owner out of Middletown. He served as judge of the Hartford County Court, colonel in the Connecticut militia, and as a selectman of Middletown. In 1756 he sat on a committee reviewing the circumstances of Cushoy, the elderly and impoverished Wangunk sachem, and made recommendations to the Connecticut General Assembly — one of the rare documented encounters between the Talcott family and indigenous Connecticut in the colonial record.

narrative_blocks.header: Commanded a regiment in the Revolution at 63
narrative_blocks.body: When the Revolution broke out Matthew Talcott was already in his sixties. He commanded Col. Matthew Talcott's Regiment at New York from February to April 1776, among the earliest Connecticut deployments of the war. He outlived the Revolution entirely, dying in 1802 at roughly 89 years old.

narrative_blocks.header: A nephew was named for him — and he paid for his education
narrative_blocks.body: Matthew Talcott Russell, born 1761, was named by his father Rev. Noadiah Russell after Colonel Matthew Talcott — who had married his aunt Mary Russell and had no children of his own. Colonel Talcott met the expenses of his nephew's education. Matthew Talcott Russell went on to study law with Oliver Ellsworth, become State's Attorney for Middlesex County, and serve as a Yale tutor.

narrative_blocks.header: He raised his future sister-in-law's husband
narrative_blocks.body: When Rev. Daniel Wadsworth died in 1747, leaving four-year-old Jeremiah fatherless, Matthew Talcott took the boy in. Jeremiah grew up in Matthew's Middletown household calling him uncle. In 1764 Jeremiah married Mehitable Russell — the sister of Matthew's own wife Mary. The guardian became the brother-in-law.

narrative_blocks.header: Commissary General to Washington — and architect of the supply chain that won the Revolution
narrative_blocks.summary: Jeremiah Wadsworth served as Commissary General of the Continental Army from April 1778, resigning in December 1779 — the period that included Valley Forge's aftermath and the French alliance. He then supplied Rochambeau's French army through Yorktown. Washington made his house his Hartford home on every wartime visit; Wadsworth was present when Benedict Arnold's treason was first discovered. He turned a substantial personal profit in the process — and freed his enslaved man Peleg Nott around 1780.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost his seat to Pierpont Edwards — who then gave it back
narrative_blocks.summary: In 1790 Jeremiah Wadsworth lost his Congressional reelection to Pierpont Edwards, fellow Hartford figure and Hooker descendant. Edwards then declined to take the seat, forcing a special election — which Wadsworth won. Two men from the same tight Hartford circle, both connected to Thomas Hooker's tree, trading the same Congressional seat in successive elections.

narrative_blocks.header: Washington slept here — and learned of Arnold's treason leaving
narrative_blocks.summary: On September 20-22, 1780, Washington and Rochambeau held their war strategy conference at Wadsworth's Hartford home — Washington arriving with Hamilton, Knox, and Lafayette. The conference ended abruptly when news arrived of Benedict Arnold's treason at West Point. Washington departed Hartford on September 23 and learned the full details of Arnold's betrayal en route. Wadsworth's house was the room where the alliance that won the Revolution was cemented — and where it nearly fell apart.

research_notes:
b. Jul 12, 1743 Hartford CT; d. Apr 30, 1804 Hartford CT age 60. Son of Rev. Daniel Wadsworth (Yale 1726) and Abigail Talcott (T00068), daughter of Gov. Joseph Talcott (T00045). RAISED BY HIS UNCLE MATTHEW TALCOTT (T00070/X00127) of Middletown — the same Matthew Talcott who married his future wife's sister Mary Russell (H00356). Shipped before the mast in his uncle's vessels to improve health; rose to mate and captain. 
REVOLUTIONARY WAR SERVICE: Commissary for American troops from 1775. COMMISSARY GENERAL FOR THE FRENCH ARMY: When Rochambeau arrived July 11, 1780, Wadsworth personally assumed responsibility as purchasing agent for all French forces. 
THE HARTFORD CONFERENCE, SEPTEMBER 20-22, 1780: Washington and Rochambeau held their strategy conference AT WADSWORTH'S HOME in Hartford. Washington brought Hamilton, Knox, Lafayette, Lt. Col. Gouvion, and 40 horses. Hans Axel von Fersen (Rochambeau's aide, later famous in Swedish history) described Washington from this meeting: "noble and majestic, but at the same time gentle and honest face... the air of a hero... very cold, speaks little, but is polite and civil." The conference ended abruptly when news arrived of Arnold's treason at West Point. Washington departed Hartford Sep 23 and learned of Arnold's treachery en route. Source: Founders Archives, GW Papers, https://founders.archives.gov/documents/Washington/03-28-02-0173-0001 
THE WETHERSFIELD CONFERENCE, MAY 1781: Washington and Rochambeau met again at Wethersfield CT to confirm plans for joining forces outside New York. 
FRENCH ARMY MARCH, JUNE-JULY 1781: By late April 1781, Wadsworth had received the French infantry's campsite list and began collecting provisions: 855 horses, 600+ oxen, 500 artillery horses, 239 wagon conductors, 15 female cooks for 210 wagons in 15 brigades. He employed men to build bread ovens along the march route from Newport to the Hudson. This logistical operation, entirely managed by Wadsworth, made the Yorktown campaign possible. Source: NPS Washington-Rochambeau Revolutionary Route, https://www.nps.gov/waro/learn/historyculture/washington-rochambeau-revolutionary-route.htm 
CONGRESS: 1st, 2nd, 3rd Congress (1789-1795), Federalist. Honorary degrees Yale and Dartmouth 1796. A French traveler (1788): "universally known for the service he rendered the American and French armies during the war." m. Mehitable Russell (H00362) Nov 18, 1764 Hartford CT.
EDWARD HOOKER 1909 NOTE: "famous Commissary-General of the Revolutionary War, and a great financier, a friend of Alexander Hamilton." "One of the few men to whom the Revolutionary War proved a source of wealth." Hamilton connection: Wadsworth was a close ally of Hamilton in financial matters — both were early advocates of the Bank of New York and federal financial architecture. POLITICAL OFFICES: U.S. Congressman (Connecticut) 1789-1795; Delegate to Continental Congress; Member CT State Legislature. Buried Ancient Burying Ground, Hartford CT. SIBLINGS OF JEREMIAH: Abigail Wadsworth (b. Jan 28, 1735; d. Apr 20, 1785 Norwich CT, buried Old Norwichtown Cemetery); Elizabeth Wadsworth (b. Jun 19, 1738; d. Nov 15, 1810, buried Ancient Burying Ground Hartford CT). Father of Daniel Wadsworth (HD1330, founder of Wadsworth Atheneum). Notable: Commissary General through nearly entire Revolutionary War; intimate friend of Washington. 2nd President Bank of New York 1785-86. Continental Congress 1788. Constitutional ratifying convention 1788. U.S. House of Representatives 1789-95 (3 terms) — lost reelection 1790 but Pierpont Edwards (H00386, fellow Hooker descendant) declined the seat and Wadsworth was reelected. Treaty Commissioner (Treaty of Big Tree with Seneca, 1797). slaveholder (documented — owned family incl. Peleg Nott, freed ~1780; 6,600-acre SC plantation). Talcott descendant through mother Abigail Talcott (T00068, dau. of Gov. Joseph Talcott T00045). Married H00362 (Mehitable Russell, gen 4 Hooker descendant) — technically I-prefix by schema but already assigned X00417. TALCOTT COUSINS WITH ELIJAH WADSWORTH (TD0059): Both descend from T00035 (Lt.-Col. John Talcott Jr. b.1632). Jeremiah through T00045 (Gov. Joseph Talcott) → T00068 (Abigail Talcott); Elijah through T00040 (Elizabeth Talcott) → TD0001 → TD0058. ~3rd cousins once removed. NO documented specific interaction confirmed — Jeremiah as Commissary General would have supplied Sheldon's Horse (Elijah's regiment) but no direct documented exchange known.
CC NOTE [H00386]: In 1790 Pierpont Edwards defeated Jeremiah Wadsworth for the Connecticut Congressional seat — then declined to take it, forcing a special election which Wadsworth won. Two men from the same tight Hartford circle, both connected to Thomas Hooker's tree, trading the same Congressional seat in successive elections. (Wikipedia — Jeremiah Wadsworth)
CC NOTE [H00348]: Jeremiah Wadsworth and Capt. James Hooker were first cousins once removed through their shared Talcott ancestry — both descended from Gov. Joseph Talcott. During the Revolution, both supplied Continental Army troops from the same Connecticut River Valley network: James through the Hooker and Chaffee firm; Jeremiah as Commissary General for the entire allied Franco-American force.
CC NOTE [H00030]: Edward Hooker 1909 (entry 62) explicitly documents that Jeremiah Wadsworth descended from Elizabeth Standley, sister of Abigail Standley who married John Hooker. This Standley cousinship existed independently of his marriage to Mehitable Russell, who was herself a Hooker descendant. When he married her, EH notes they were already distant cousins through both the Standley bloodline and the Hooker bloodline simultaneously.

narrative_blocks.header: First Mayor of New York — appointed 1665
narrative_blocks.body: Thomas Willet was appointed the first Mayor of New York City by Governor Richard Nicolls in 1665, shortly after the English took the city from the Dutch. He had joined the English expedition against New Amsterdam the previous year alongside Governor Winthrop of Connecticut.

narrative_blocks.body: A trusted Plymouth Colony leader, Willet succeeded the legendary Miles Standish as Captain of the Plymouth military company in 1648. He had arrived in Plymouth on the ship Lion in 1632, associated with the Leyden congregation.

narrative_blocks.header: Arrived 1637 with Buckinghamshire neighbors — not 1630 on the Mary and John
narrative_blocks.summary: Online sources commonly place Benjamin Fenn's arrival on the Mary and John in 1630, but he never appears in colonial records until 1637 — a seven-year gap that makes the 1630 date implausible. A more likely scenario: he traveled in 1637 with a group of families from the Buckinghamshire area.
narrative_blocks.detail: After a brief stop in Dorchester, Massachusetts, Fenn settled at Milford, Connecticut, where he received lot #3 (2 acres, 11 rods, 3 poles) and remained for the rest of his life. He married Sarah Baldwin, reportedly the daughter of Sylvester Baldwin, about 1638. Sarah's father Sylvester wrote his will on June 21, 1638 on board the ship Martin while crossing the Atlantic — admitted to probate in Boston shortly after arrival — placing the Baldwin family in the same 1638 emigration wave as Fenn. Fenn's English estate in Buckinghamshire, valued at £600 sterling, was left to his son Samuel in his will; there is no evidence he ever returned to claim it. (Empty Branches on the Family Tree; Families of Early Milford; EH 1909)

narrative_blocks.header: Deputy, Magistrate, and Assistant Governor — one of Milford's most prominent founders
narrative_blocks.summary: Benjamin Fenn served Milford and the Connecticut Colony for nearly two decades: as Deputy to the General Assembly (1653–54), Magistrate of Milford (1654–63), Commissioner for the United Colonies (1661, 1663), and finally as Assistant to the Governor of Connecticut Colony (1665–1672).
narrative_blocks.detail: Fenn was admitted to the Milford church in September 1640 alongside his wife Sarah. He served with Governor William Leete as a Commissioner for the United Colonies of New England in 1661 and 1663 — the same Governor Leete whose son William Jr. would later marry Fenn's daughter Mary. A local DAR chapter at Milford is named in his honor. His son-in-law connection to the Leete family, combined with his daughter Martha's marriage into the Newton-Hooker line and his daughter Susanna's marriage to William Hooker (H00029), made Benjamin Fenn a nexus of Milford's first generation. He died September 14, 1672; inventory taken November 13, 1672; probated February 1, 1674. (EH 1909; Families of Early Milford; Empty Branches)

narrative_blocks.header: 'Hart's Ford' — the tradition that Hartford was named for Stephen Hart
narrative_blocks.summary: According to a tradition recorded in 1910, when Hooker's company crossed the Connecticut River, Hart discovered a ford where the water was low. The settlement at that crossing became 'Hart's Ford' — and Hartford followed. His descendant Almira Hart Lincoln Phelps put it in verse c.1871: 'Yes, Hartford has a charm — its name derived, / As legends say, from Stephen Hart.'
narrative_blocks.detail: The poem was read publicly by Dr. Henry Barnard. The tradition cannot be confirmed by documentary evidence, but it was accepted locally for generations and is part of Hartford's founding lore. Hart was admitted freeman at the same session as Thomas Hooker and Samuel Stone — May 14, 1634 — and came to Hartford with Hooker's company in 1636 as an original proprietor. (Hart Family Genealogy; EH 1909)

narrative_blocks.header: One of the Seven Pillars — founding deacon of Farmington church, 1652
narrative_blocks.summary: When the Farmington church was organized on November 1652, seven men stepped forward as its founding pillars. Stephen Hart, already a deacon of Hooker's Hartford church, was named the town's first deacon. The other six pillars were Rev. Roger Newton, John Cole, John Bronson, Robert Porter, Thomas Judd, and Thomas Thompson.
narrative_blocks.detail: Hart had purchased a large tract on the border of what is now Avon CT — 'Hart's Farm,' which still bears his name. His house lot on the west side of Main Street was 15 acres, granted on condition he continue running the mill on the property. He represented Farmington at the General Court for 15 sessions (1647–1655) and once in 1660. He died in March 1682/3, leaving an estate that named his grandchild John Hart — the future sole survivor of the 1666 Indian fire — a bequest of £3. (EH 1909; Hart Family Genealogy)

narrative_blocks.header: The 1666 Indian fire — an entire family destroyed, one grandson surviving by chance
narrative_blocks.summary: In December 1666, Indians set fire to the Hart house in Farmington in the night. All of Stephen's son John's family perished in the flames — except John Jr., who was that night at Nod (Northington/Avon) looking after stock. The burning also destroyed Farmington's town records. The General Court made diligent search among the Tunxis tribe for the incendiaries.
narrative_blocks.detail: John Jr.'s survival — accidental, providential — preserved the Hart line. His son became Capt. John Hart (X00031), whose three sons each married a daughter of John Hooker (H00030), Thomas Hooker's grandson. The fire that destroyed one generation became the pivot that connected the Hart and Hooker families in the next. Stephen Hart's will (1682) specifically named 'my grandchild John Hart, my eldest son's son' — acknowledging the boy's fragile place in the line. (EH 1909; Hart Family Genealogy)

research_notes:
b. ~1605 Braintree, Essex, England (EH; some sources say 1603 Ipswich). d. Mar 1682/3 Farmington CT age ~77 (will Mar 16; inventory Mar 31, 1682/3). HARTFORD FOUNDER. THOMAS HOOKER'S DEACON. Came to Cambridge/Newtown ~1632 with the "Braintree Company." Admitted freeman May 14, 1634 — same session as Thomas Hooker and Samuel Stone. Had been deacon of Rev. Thomas Hooker's church at Cambridge AND Hartford. Came to Hartford with Hooker's company 1635. Original proprietor of Hartford 1639. One of 84 proprietors of Farmington 1672. One of seven pillars of Farmington church, organized Nov 1652; first deacon. Other pillars: Rev. Roger Newton (Hooker's student, married his daughter), John Cole, John Bronson, Robert Porter, Thomas Judd, Thomas Thompson. Deputy to CT General Court 1647-1655, 1660. Largest house-lot in Farmington (15 acres) granted as inducement to build mill. "Hart's Farm" in Avon still bears his name; "Hart's Ford" tradition for Hartford. LAND DESCENT: Thomas Hart (son) → Josiah Hart → daughter married Roger Hooker → Thomas Hart Hooker (only surviving child) — Hooker-Hart land connection documented. HOOKER CONNECTION: Grandson Capt. John Hart (X00031): three sons each married a daughter of John Hooker (H00030), Thomas Hooker's grandson. m.(1) [Elizabeth/Unknown] — name not recorded; d. by ~1678 Farmington. m.(2) Margaret, widow of Arthur Smith (d.1655) and Joseph Nash (d.1678); admitted church Farmington Mar 17, 1690/1; d. 1693 Farmington. (EH section on Stephen Hart; Hart Family Genealogy)

narrative_blocks.header: More than any man — Waterbury's founding father
narrative_blocks.body: Bronson wrote of him: "He was, more than any other man, with the exception of Thomas Judd senior, the ruling spirit and father of the settlement." He laid out lots, staked fences, located highways, and settled town boundaries — and as first Recorder, his was the only fully legible and business-like hand among Waterbury's earliest proprietors.

narrative_blocks.header: His manuscripts preserved Waterbury's founding records
narrative_blocks.body: His handwritten folio books — copied for preservation in 1705 by town appointment — survived into the nineteenth century and became the primary source for Waterbury's founding history. His son Nathaniel's account-book continued the family's role as the town's unofficial archivists.

narrative_blocks.header: Left Waterbury suddenly — no one knew why
narrative_blocks.body: In a time of great affliction, John Stanley quietly quit the town he had done more than any other man to build. Bronson wrote in 1858: he could not say what persuaded Stanley to leave, adding: 'It is to be hoped he had a better reason than any that can be thought of at this distant day. His loss must have been seriously felt.' He returned to Farmington and never explained himself.

narrative_blocks.header: The only man to govern both New Haven Colony and the Colony of Connecticut
narrative_blocks.body: He served Guilford as town clerk from 1639, rose to Governor of New Haven Colony (1661-1664), and then Governor of the Colony of Connecticut (1676-1683) — the only person ever to hold both offices. He is buried at the Ancient Burying Ground in Hartford — the same ground as Thomas Hooker.

narrative_blocks.header: He used the Sabbath and slow horses to save the regicides
narrative_blocks.body: When royal agents arrived demanding the regicides Goffe and Whalley, he read the King's warrant aloud in public — alerting everyone to the agents' purpose — then furnished horses so slowly that the Sabbath intervened before they could depart. By Monday the men were hidden. Roger Newton did the same in Milford; Roger Hooker did the same in Hartford — three men in the same civic network, the same year.

narrative_blocks.header: His granddaughters married into the Hooker family — two branches
narrative_blocks.body: His son William Leete Jr. fathered Mary Leete, who married Hon. James Hooker (H00031) of Guilford. His son Andrew Leete fathered Mercy Leete, who married Samuel Hooker (H00086) of Kensington. Two of his granddaughters independently married into the Hooker family in the same generation.

narrative_blocks.body: On May 4, 1661, Miles Merwin was received into the Milford Church personally by Rev. Roger Newton, its founding pastor. His third wife Sarah Platt was a daughter of Deacon Richard Platt, another Milford founding settler — placing him at the intersection of Milford's most prominent founding families.

narrative_blocks.header: His daughters were first cousins to the Platt sisters who married the Newton cousins
narrative_blocks.body: His third wife Sarah Platt was daughter of Deacon Richard Platt; Joseph Platt (X00076) was Sarah's brother. Joseph's daughters Phebe and Hannah Platt each married a Newton first cousin. The Merwin daughters and Platt daughters were thus first cousins — all woven into the same tight circle of Milford families.
