Built a mill ca.1645 on site later occupied by Eli Whitney's cotton gin factory (~2 miles from New Haven). Town bought mill Jan 3, 1659 for £100. Chosen successively Sergeant, Lieutenant, and Captain of the Town Company. "Fowler's Cove" and "Fowler's Creek" near East Haven named for him.

EASTER EGG: William Fowler of New Haven and Milford — one of the "Seven Pillars" of the Milford church; magistrate of New Haven Colony 1643; built the mill later occupied by Eli Whitney's cotton gin factory; brother-in-law of Governor Robert Treat through his wife's sister Jane Tapp.

cross_connections.display_label: Both destroyed the Wepawaug fort — fined £10.

narrative_blocks.header: Destroyed a Wepawaug fort — fined £10
narrative_blocks.body: In 1671, John Fowler was among the young men of Milford who destroyed the Wepawaug Indian fort — an act of colonial mischief for which he and Joseph Platt (X00076) were tried by the New Haven General Court, fined £10 each, and ordered to rebuild it.

narrative_blocks.header: Killed taking an Indian fort — 1693
narrative_blocks.body: John Fowler died around 1693 in the taking of an Indian fort during King William's War. His will named his "only son John" and called Governor Robert Treat his "honorable uncle." His widow Sarah remarried Capt. Samuel Newton four years later.

research_notes:
DEATH NOTE: Died before 1695 — in a deed of that date Sarah Fowler is called "widow and executor." Will probated 1693; names "only son John" and "my honorable uncle, Robert Treat, Esq., the Governor."
WILL (1693): Names "only son John" (I00091). Wife to raise him in fear of God. Calls Gov. Robert Treat "my honorable uncle" — Treat was uncle by marriage through William Fowler's wife Mary Tapp. Probated 1693.
ANECDOTE (wepawaug fort): John Fowler was one of the "young men" of Milford who in 1671 destroyed the Wepawaug Indian fort — the same prank for which Joseph Platt (X00076) and friends were tried by the General Court at New Haven and fined £10 each. Both men are now X-tier ancestors in this tree through separate lines. The Wepawaug fort incident connects the Fowler and Platt families in the same act of colonial mischief, twelve years before their descendants would marry into the Newton family.

Peter Prudden — the same company as William Fowler (X00095), Edmund Tapp, Thomas Buckingham, and Thomas Welch.

Hertfordshire neighbor of Hooker's Cambridge circle; adjoined Prudden.

narrative_blocks.header: He left a Bible to each of his nineteen grandchildren
narrative_blocks.body: When Deacon Richard Platt made his will in 1683, he bequeathed a Bible to each of his nineteen grandchildren — a gesture that, as one historian noted, "betrays a profound interest in the best things in life" in rough pioneer days. His estate was valued at approximately £600 sterling.

narrative_blocks.header: From Rev. Chauncey's congregation in Ware to founding Milford's church
narrative_blocks.body: Richard Platt grew up in Ware, Hertfordshire — a hotbed of Puritanism where the famous Rev. Charles Chauncey (later president of Harvard) preached from 1627 to 1633. Deeply influenced by Chauncey's teaching, he came to New England in 1638 with Rev. Peter Prudden's Hertfordshire company, and became one of 66 founding members of the Milford church on August 22, 1639.

narrative_blocks.header: His name is on Milford's Founder's Bridge — over the Wepawaug River
narrative_blocks.body: The Platt stone on the Wepawaug Memorial Bridge in Milford reads: "Deacon Richard Platt, Obit 1684 — Mary His Wife." He was chosen Deacon of First Church Milford in 1669, witnessed the will of Rev. Peter Prudden, and appears as a witness in the colony records. He is buried in his orchard at lot #38, West Main and Cherry Streets, Milford.

narrative_blocks.header: Nine children — their descendants married throughout the Newton-Hooker circle
narrative_blocks.body: His nine children — Mary, John, Isaac, Samuel, Sarah, Epenetus, Hannah, Josiah, and Joseph — spread through the families of Milford and surrounding towns. Daughter Mary married Luke Atkinson; daughter Sarah married Thomas Beach then Miles Merwin; son Joseph married Mary Kellogg. Their descendants appear as spouses throughout the Newton-Hooker family network across three generations.

narrative_blocks.header: He left his entire estate to son Joseph — "I make my son Joseph my executor"
narrative_blocks.body: His will left Joseph Platt the entirety of his estate: "I give my son Joseph Platt all the remainder of my estate... I make my son Joseph Platt my executor." The family had emigrated from Hertfordshire, England in 1639, helping found New Haven and Milford. Joseph was the first Platt child born in America.

research_notes:
OCCUPATION NOTES: Probably educated at the Grammar School at Ware; apprenticed in some trade by 1629. Described as "Richard Platt of Ware" in apprentice court records 1629. His father and grandfather were tailors.

EASTER EGG: Deacon Richard Platt — Milford founding settler 1639; Deacon of First Church Milford 1669; his descendants married into at least five separate Hooker-connected households across three generations. His name is on the coping stones of the Wepawaug Memorial Bridge in Milford, commemorating the early settlers. | [SOURCE MOVED] Families of Early Milford CT — Ancestry.com: https://www.ancestry.com/imageviewer/collections/48021/images/FamMilfordCT-007630-496

classification.notes: Exceptional scope entry. First minister of Milford 1640-1656. Came with Hertfordshire company including Richard Platt (X00098) and William Fowler (X00095). Roger Newton Sr. (I00003) succeeded him at Milford in 1660. His great-great-grandson John Prudden (I00084) married Hannah Newton (H00153), great-great-granddaughter of Thomas Hooker.

narrative_blocks.header: He negotiated Milford's land and founded its church
narrative_blocks.body: In February 1639, Prudden's group purchased land called Wepawaug from the Paugassetts, naming the settlement Milford after a stream that could power a mill. Six months later he gathered fifteen families into the First Church of Christ, and was ordained its first pastor in April 1640.

narrative_blocks.header: Buried in his own garden — which became the town cemetery
narrative_blocks.body: Peter Prudden died in August 1656 and was buried in his garden on Prospect Street alongside an infant son. The approximately seven-acre property became Milford's first graveyard. John Winthrop wrote that he "was useful in his place and of high esteem in the colony."

narrative_blocks.header: His widow married the first Mayor of New York
narrative_blocks.body: After Prudden's death, his widow Joanna married Captain Thomas Willett, who became the first Mayor of New York City. Their daughter Mary Willet then married Reverend Samuel Hooker, connecting the Prudden and Hooker founding families.

research_notes: EASTER EGG: Rev. Samuel Hooker (H00007).

narrative_blocks.header: Co-founded Yale — secured the charter in 1701
narrative_blocks.body: He was the principal organizer of the Collegiate School of Connecticut, chartered in 1701 — which took the surname of its chief benefactor Elihu Yale. He served as founding trustee from October 16, 1701 until his death in 1714, and as pastor of First Church New Haven from 1685 to 1714, a period of 29 years.

narrative_blocks.header: Three wives — first died of a cold
narrative_blocks.body: He married Abigail Davenport (daughter of New Haven's founder Rev. John Davenport) on October 27, 1691; she died February 3, 1692 from a cold caught shortly after. He married Sarah Haynes in 1694 — she died the same day their daughter Abigail was born, October 27, 1696. In 1698 he married Mary Hooker, granddaughter of Rev. Thomas Hooker — she bore him eight children and outlived him by 26 years.

narrative_blocks.header: His children's lines: Burr and J.P. Morgan
narrative_blocks.body: His daughter Sarah Pierpont married Jonathan Edwards; their grandson Aaron Burr became the 3rd Vice President of the United States and killed Alexander Hamilton in a duel in 1804. His son James II's line leads to financier J.P. Morgan. His descendants also include songwriter James Lord Pierpont, who wrote "Jingle Bells," and politician Edwards Pierrepont.

narrative_blocks.header: 1706 ordination — five ministers, all Hooker-tree
narrative_blocks.body: On November 25, 1706, he was among five ministers who gathered to ordain Rev. John Hart at East Guilford — along with Rev. Thomas Buckingham of Saybrook, Rev. Noadiah Russell of Middletown, Rev. Samuel Russell of Branford, and Rev. Thomas Ruggles of Guilford. Hart was Yale Class of 1703, the first student to receive a Yale B.A. All five ministers' names run through the Hooker genealogical tree.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale portrait — painted the year before death
narrative_blocks.body: His portrait was painted in 1711 by the Pierpont Limner, three years before his death. It is held at the Yale University Art Gallery (accession no. 1969.62.1), bequeathed by Allen Evarts Foster, B.A. 1906. He lived in New Haven at what was known as the Pierpont Mansion.

research_notes:
b. Jan 4, 1659 Roxbury MA; d. Nov 22, 1714 New Haven CT age 55. Son of John Pierpont (b. London 1619; Roxbury town officer; d.1682) and Thankful Stow Pierpont (1629-1664, daughter of John Stow). Roxbury Latin School; Harvard 1681. Ordained Jul 2, 1685; minister First Church New Haven 1685-1714. YALE FOUNDER: Secured charter for Collegiate School of Connecticut 1701; founding trustee Oct 16, 1701 until death 1714. NOT A HOOKER DESCENDANT — married into the Hooker line via third wife Mary Hooker (H00034). m.(1) Abigail Davenport (X01868) Oct 27, 1691 — d. Feb 3, 1692, no children. m.(2) Sarah Haynes (X01869) May 30, 1694 — d. Oct 27, 1696 (same day Abigail born). m.(3) Mary Hooker (H00034) Jul 26, 1698 — d. Nov 1, 1740; eight children. Portrait: ART039, Pierpont Limner 1711, Yale Art Gallery accession 1969.62.1.

narrative_blocks.header: Ruling elder of Milford church — 45 years
narrative_blocks.body: He was named ruling elder of the First Church of Christ in Milford on June 4, 1667, and held that position until his death on May 2, 1712 — 45 years of continuous service, the last person ever to hold that role. His civic partners included Major Robert Treat, later Governor of Connecticut, and Samuel Eels.

narrative_blocks.header: Named executor of his brother-in-law's will in 1708
narrative_blocks.body: In 1708 he was named executor of the estate of Samuel Newton — his wife Alice's brother, and grandson of Rev. Thomas Hooker. He had married Alice Newton in 1689 as his second wife, connecting the Buckingham and Hooker families at Milford.

career.notes: Named ruling elder June 4, 1667. Served until his death in 1712 — the last person to hold that role.

research_notes:
b. c.Aug 6, 1636 England. Arrived Boston Jul 31, 1637 as infant. d. May 2, 1712 Milford CT. Buried Milford Cemetery. Gravestone: "Here Lyes Buried Ye Body of Mr Daniel Buckingham Aged 75 years Dec'd May Ye 2d 1712." Father: Thomas Buckingham (X02133), Milford founder. m.(1) Nov 21, 1661 Hannah Fowler (dau. William Fowler and Mary Tapp). Hannah was still alive May 4, 1673 (church member, full communion). Chapman says she died June 24, 1667 but this is DISPROVED by the 1673 church record. Bore Josiah in 1677; died between 1677 and 1689 (before Daniel's remarriage to Alice). Eight children by Hannah: Hannah(1663), Daniel(1665), Esther(1667,d.infancy), Mary(1668), Thomas(1670), John(1673), Gideon(1675), Josiah(1677). Not yet entered. m.(2) 1689 Alice Newton (H00018). No children with Alice. Named executor Samuel Newton will 1708 (H00011, Alice's brother). CIVIC: Sargeant 1673; overseer Sanford estate 1681; built town mill with Robert Treat 1674, 1702; named executor H00011 Samuel Newton 1708.
MILITARY: Listed as "Sargeant Daniel Buckingham" in 1673 court records. FindAGrave lists him as VETERAN. Likely served in King Philip's War (1675-1676) given his rank, but specific war record not yet confirmed. No military tag applied until confirmed.

narrative_blocks.header: His uncle hid Connecticut's Charter in the Oak
narrative_blocks.summary: Rev. Daniel Wadsworth's uncle Joseph Wadsworth was the man who seized the Connecticut Charter on the night of October 31, 1687 and hid it in the Charter Oak in Hartford — aided by Lt.-Col. John Talcott, the grandfather of Daniel's future wife Abigail Talcott. Daniel himself had no part in it; he was born 17 years later. But both families whose founding myth was the Charter Oak — Wadsworth and Talcott — converged in his household.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at 43 — his son was four years old
narrative_blocks.summary: Daniel Wadsworth died on November 12, 1747, when his son Jeremiah was four years old. The boy was sent to be raised by his Talcott uncle Matthew Talcott of Middletown — his mother Abigail's brother. The man who would become Commissary General of the Continental Army and intimate friend of Washington grew up in a Talcott household, knowing his father's family only by reputation.

education.institution_blurb: Yale College 1726. Member of Yale Corporation 1743 until his death in 1747.

research_notes:
b. Nov 14, 1704; d. Nov 12, 1747 Hartford CT age 43. Son of Deacon John Wadsworth of Farmington and Elizabeth Stanley. Yale College 1726; member of Yale Corporation 1743 until death. Pastor, First Congregational Church, Hartford CT, Sep 28, 1732 (succeeded Rev. Timothy Woodbridge, who died Apr 30, 1732). m. Abigail Talcott (T00068) Feb 28, 1733-4 — daughter of Gov. Joseph Talcott. ANCESTRY: His father Deacon John Wadsworth (b. Apr 14, 1662 in England; d. 1689) married Elizabeth, daughter of John Stanley, Aug 20, 1696. Deacon John's father John Wadsworth Sr. came from England, was married to Sarah Stanley (daughter of Thomas Stanley). Joseph Wadsworth, Deacon John's brother, "on the night of the 31 Oct. 1687, aided by Lt.-Col. John Talcott, seized the Charter of Connecticut given by Charles 2d and secured it in an Oak tree in Hartford." (CHARTER OAK.) So Daniel Wadsworth's uncle was the man who hid the Connecticut Charter. Source: Talcott Pedigree entry 68. Parents confirmed: Deacon John Wadsworth (X00454) and Elizabeth Standley (X00453), daughter of Capt. John Standley and sister of Abigail Standley who married Hon. John Hooker (H00030).
CC NOTE [H00030]: Daniel's mother Elizabeth Standley was the sister of Abigail Standley, who married Hon. John Hooker of Farmington. The two families were Standley kin before the Hooker-Wadsworth connection through marriage. Documented explicitly in EH 1909 entry 62.

CC NOTE [I00031]: Elizabeth Standley and her sister Abigail Standley each married into families that became deeply intertwined with the Hooker legacy: Abigail married Hon. John Hooker directly into the Thomas Hooker line; Elizabeth married Deacon John Wadsworth, whose grandson Col. Jeremiah Wadsworth then married Mehitable Russell — herself a Hooker descendant. The two Standley sisters thus created two parallel threads connecting to Thomas Hooker's descendants, which converged in the marriage of Jeremiah Wadsworth and Mehitable Russell.

narrative_blocks.header: Died of consumption weeks after landing — February 1636
narrative_blocks.body: She sailed with Thomas Shepard and their infant son on the Defence, arriving at Boston on October 3, 1635. She died of consumption at Cambridge in February 1636, weeks after landing. Thomas Shepard recorded her death in his autobiography. She was roughly 29 years old. Their surviving son Thomas later became a minister.

narrative_blocks.header: Hosted the Saybrook Platform in his own house
narrative_blocks.body: On September 9, 1708, the Connecticut Synod assembled at his Saybrook home: twelve ministers, four laymen, eight of the twelve being Yale Trustees. The result was the Saybrook Platform — the document that defined Connecticut Congregationalism for the next century, a carefully negotiated middle way between Massachusetts and Presbyterian practice.

narrative_blocks.header: One of twelve original Trustees of Yale College
narrative_blocks.body: He was among the twelve ministers who signed Yale's founding documents in 1701. He died April 1, 1709 — "second in age among the Trustees, and for forty-three years pastor of Saybrook." His son Stephen received Yale's first academic degree at its first commencement.

narrative_blocks.header: Her grandfather founded Springfield — and was burned
narrative_blocks.body: Her mother Ann Pynchon was the daughter of William Pynchon — who founded Springfield, Massachusetts, and whose 1650 book was the first to be publicly burned in New England. Mary carried one of colonial New England's most prominent lineages when she married Rev. Thomas Hooker's grandson.

narrative_blocks.header: First husband drowned — she ran the estate
narrative_blocks.body: Her first husband Richard Lord II, son of Hartford founder Richard Lord, was lost at sea on November 5, 1685, aged 49. Mary petitioned the court; in December 1686 she was awarded the personal estate, one-third of the real estate, and management of the Lord estate until her son Richard Lord III came of age. She managed it alone for seven years before marrying Thomas Hooker in 1686.

parents.father_research_notes: X01001 married I00006 Mary Willet (widow of H00007 Samuel Hooker) in 1703. So Stephen's father became the stepfather of Stephen's wife Sarah's mother.

narrative_blocks.header: First name in Yale's first Triennial Catalogue
narrative_blocks.body: At Yale's first commencement in 1702, Stephen received his second academic degree — the first awarded by the new Collegiate School. His name appears first in the Triennial Catalogue of Yale College, making him the college's first officially recorded graduate, despite having taken his first degree at Harvard in 1693.

narrative_blocks.header: His will left everything to his wife Sarah
narrative_blocks.body: Stephen's will, dated January 1, 1745/46 and proved February 18 in Fairfield, was simple and complete: he left everything to his wife Sarah. They had been married nearly sixty years.

narrative_blocks.header: His nephew died visiting at 24
narrative_blocks.body: Jedidiah Buckingham — Stephen's nephew — visited him in Norwalk in 1720. He had recently left his pulpit in Newark, New Jersey. He died at Stephen's house March 28, 1720, aged 24, and was buried in Norwalk.

narrative_blocks.header: Cleared of charges — then pushed out anyway
narrative_blocks.body: After thirty years at Norwalk, Buckingham was charged with intemperance. A Consociation investigated and found no fault in his conduct. The town then found a letter they could construe as a resignation and accepted it before he could object. He appealed to the Assembly; the outcome is not recorded. He gave his 200-acre farm on Buckingham Ridge to his orphaned nephew, and died in 1746 — in the words of the Wilton historian — "a saddened and embittered man."

education.notes: H.U. 1693. Also member of the Corporation of Yale College.

narrative_blocks.body: She married Rev. James Pierpont Sr. on October 27, 1691, in New Haven. She died on February 3, 1692 — roughly three weeks later — from a cold caught shortly after the ceremony. She was 19 years old. She was the granddaughter of Rev. John Davenport Sr. (X02123), who had co-founded New Haven Colony in 1638 and envisioned a college there.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale Fellow and Guilford's founding pastor — present at the ordination of Rev. John Hart alongside James Pierpont, Noadiah Russell, and Samuel Russell
narrative_blocks.summary: Thomas Ruggles was pastor of First Church Guilford from 1695 until his death in 1728 and a Fellow of Yale College from 1710 — becoming the first Trustee to serve while technically underage (he hadn't yet reached 40, as the charter required). He was present at the November 25, 1706 ordination of Rev. John Hart over East Guilford's congregation, alongside Yale founders James Pierpont, Noadiah Russell, Samuel Russell, and Thomas Buckingham — all names woven into the Hooker genealogical tree.
narrative_blocks.detail: His son Dr. Nathaniel Ruggles (X00282) married Annie Bartlett (H00317), a 4th-generation Hooker descendant through the Hooker-Bartlett line, connecting the Ruggles family to the Hooker tree. His daughter married Rev. Thomas Ruggles Jr. (Y.C. 1723), who later gave the funeral sermon for the eminent physician-clergyman Jared Eliot. (Dexter, Yale Biographical Sketches; Yale Annals)

education.institution_blurb: Fellow of Yale College 1710-1728; present at East Guilford ordination 1706.

narrative_blocks.header: Fled England — founded New Haven Colony, 1638
narrative_blocks.body: Left England 1633, Holland, then Boston 1637. Co-founded New Haven Colony with Theophilus Eaton in 1638. Pastor First Church New Haven for thirty years. Opposed Half-Way Covenant and New Haven's annexation by Connecticut in 1664. Buried King's Chapel Burying Ground, Boston — same tomb as John Cotton.

narrative_blocks.header: Envisioned Yale — realized thirty years posthumously
narrative_blocks.body: Yale University was envisioned by Davenport, though his proposal was not realized until thirty years after his death. Rev. James Pierpont Sr. (X00126) — who married Davenport's granddaughter Abigail (X01868) — secured the Yale charter in 1701. Davenport College at Yale is named in his honor. His portrait (ART041) is the oldest work in the Yale Art Gallery collection, accession 1750.1.

research_notes: b. Apr 9, 1597 Coventry, Warwickshire, England; d. Mar 15, 1670 Boston MA (apoplexy). Son of Henry Davenport (alderman/mayor Coventry) and Winifred Barnaby. Oxford (Merton College 1613; BD/MA 1625). Ordained Congregationalist. Co-founded New Haven Colony 1638. GRANDSON connection: His son John Jr. (X02124) fathered Abigail (X01868) who married Rev. James Pierpont Sr. (X00126). is_easter_egg: true — grandfather of X01868.

cross_connections.display_label: Both New Haven founders; both rejected the Half-Way Covenant.

narrative_blocks.header: Every principle clash ended in a new town
narrative_blocks.body: He came to New England in 1639, helped found Southampton on Long Island (1640), then led a group to found Branford CT (1644), and finally Newark NJ (1666). Each move was driven by his rejection of compromise on church membership standards. At Newark he pursued the theocratic vision of New Haven Colony that John Davenport had established — both men opponents of the Half-Way Covenant.

narrative_blocks.header: Son founded Yale; daughter married its co-founder's son
narrative_blocks.body: His son Abraham Pierson Jr. (X02127) became the first rector of Yale College (1701-1707), teaching the first Yale students in his own home at Killingworth CT. His daughter Abigail Pierson (X02125) married John Davenport Jr. (X02124) — son of Rev. John Davenport Sr. (X02123), who had envisioned a college in New Haven decades before Yale was chartered. Both lines converge on the Yale founding.

research_notes: b. ca.1611 Thornton, Bradford, Yorkshire, England; d. Aug 9, 1678 Newark NJ. Trinity College, Cambridge B.A. 1632. Ordained deacon York 1632. Came to New England 1639/1640. Founded Southampton Long Island 1640, Branford CT 1644, Newark NJ 1666. Opposed Half-Way Covenant; left Branford when New Haven was annexed by Connecticut. m. Abigail Mitchell (daughter of Matthew Mitchell). Children include: Abraham Jr. (X02127, first rector of Yale 1701-07) and Abigail (X02125, married John Davenport Jr. X02124). is_easter_egg: true — father of X02125 (maternal grandmother of X01868 Abigail Davenport Pierpont) and father of X02127 (first Yale rector).

cross_connections.display_label: Newton trained him at Milford — he became Yale's first rector.
Both founding trustees of Yale 1701; Pierpont secured the charter.
First Yale tutor; Thomas Hooker's grandson.

narrative_blocks.header: Taught Yale's first class in his own home
narrative_blocks.body: Elected first rector on November 11, 1701, he could not leave his Killingworth congregation, so the first Yale students were taught in his home parsonage. The first student, Jacob Hemingway of East Haven, "solus was all the College the first half-year." By 1704 there were twenty students, most boarding at the Pierson home. He died in office on March 5, 1707.

narrative_blocks.header: First Yale tutor: Daniel Hooker, Thomas Hooker's grandson
narrative_blocks.body: The first Yale tutor he hired was Daniel Hooker (H00036) — a grandson of Rev. Thomas Hooker, founder of Hartford. Hooker also became Yale's first B.A. graduate in 1703. The second tutor, John Hart, received the first Yale B.A. in course in 1703 and was ordained at East Guilford in 1706 in a ceremony that included Rev. James Pierpont Sr. (X00126).

narrative_blocks.header: Studied theology under Rev. Roger Newton at Milford
narrative_blocks.body: After graduating Harvard 1668 he went to Milford CT to study theology under Rev. Roger Newton (I00003) — the founding pastor of Farmington, trained by Thomas Hooker himself. At Milford he also met Thomas Buckingham, who would later join him as one of the original Yale trustees.

research_notes: b. 1646 Southampton, Long Island NY; d. Mar 5, 1707 Killingworth (now Clinton) CT. Son of Rev. Abraham Pierson Elder (X02126) and Abigail Mitchell. SIBLING of Abigail Pierson Davenport (X02125). Harvard 1668. Studied theology under Rev. Roger Newton (I00003) at Milford. m. Abigail Clark at Milford. Pastorates: Newark NJ 1670-1692, Greenwich CT 1692-1694, Killingworth (Clinton) CT 1694-1707. FIRST RECTOR YALE 1701-1707: elected Nov 11, 1701. Taught classes in his home parsonage. First Yale tutor: Daniel Hooker (H00036, Thomas Hooker's grandson). Interred Clinton CT. Pierson College at Yale named for him. A statue of him stands on Old Campus at Yale. is_easter_egg: true — BROTHER of X02125, first Yale rector, connection to I00003 Roger Newton.

cross_connections.display_label: Both prosecuted Hutchinson — Shepard's voice, Wilson's verdict.

narrative_blocks.header: He excommunicated Anne Hutchinson from the pulpit
narrative_blocks.body: At the conclusion of her church trial in March 1638, Wilson delivered the final blow: "Forasmuch as you, Mrs. Hutchinson, have highly transgressed and offended...I doe cast you out and deliver you up to Sathan...I command you in the name of Christ Jesus...as a Leper to withdraw your selfe out of the Congregation." Hutchinson left the colony within a week.

narrative_blocks.header: Mary Dyer: nay, I repent not
narrative_blocks.body: On June 1, 1660, Wilson watched his former parishioner Mary Dyer approach the Boston gallows for the second time after returning to the colony against orders. He called to her: "Mary Dyer, O repent, O repent, and be not so deluded and carried away by deceit of the devil." She replied: "Nay, man, I am not now to repent." The ladder was kicked away.

narrative_blocks.header: His son married Thomas Hooker's daughter
narrative_blocks.body: His son John Wilson Jr. attended Harvard in 1642 and married Sarah Hooker, daughter of Rev. Thomas Hooker, founder of Hartford. The two men likely formed a friendship at Emmanuel College, Cambridge, years before either came to New England.

narrative_blocks.header: Called himself vile — refused any portrait
narrative_blocks.body: When urged to sit for a portrait, Wilson replied: "What! Such a poor vile creature as I am! Shall my picture be drawn? I say No; it never shall." A line drawing in the Massachusetts Historical Society was made after his death.

education.notes: Four years; chosen to speak Latin oration before duc de Biron, ambassador from Henry IV of France.
M.A. 1613. Noted for Puritan advocacy. Likely formed friendship with Thomas Hooker here.

career.notes: Chaplain to the Countess of Leicester and other prominent families.

research_notes: b. c.1588 Windsor, Berkshire, England. d. Aug 7, 1667 Boston MA. Father: Rev. William Wilson, chaplain to Archbishop Grindal. Mother: Isabel Woodhull, niece of Archbishop Grindal. Eton → King's College Cambridge (B.A. 1609/10) → Inns of Court → Emmanuel College Cambridge (M.A. 1613 — likely met Thomas Hooker here). Married Elizabeth Mansfield, daughter of Sir John Mansfield. Arrived with Winthrop Fleet 1630. First minister First Church Boston. Antinomian Controversy 1636-1638: formally excommunicated Hutchinson. Supervised execution of Mary Dyer June 1, 1660. TREE CONNECTION: His son John Wilson Jr. (I00004) married Sarah Hooker (H00006), daughter of Thomas Hooker. Wilson Sr. and Hooker likely knew each other from Emmanuel College Cambridge and confirmed their connection in New England.

burial.plot_notes: Buried in Rev. Peter Prudden's Garden — no tombstone remains. FindAGrave cenotaph at Milford Cemetery.

narrative_blocks.header: One of Milford's fifteen founding families
narrative_blocks.body: He arrived in Boston on July 31, 1637 from England as an infant with his family, part of the company that included Rev. John Davenport and Rev. Peter Prudden. On August 22, 1639, the First Church of Christ in Milford was organized, and Thomas was among the founding fifteen families and one of the church's first pillars. He died in 1657 when his son Daniel was twenty-one.

narrative_blocks.header: Son Daniel served as church elder 45 years
narrative_blocks.body: His son Daniel Buckingham was named ruling elder of the First Church of Milford in 1667, ten years after Thomas's death, and held that position until his own death in 1712 — the last person ever to serve in that role. Daniel's second wife was Alice Newton, granddaughter of Rev. Thomas Hooker.

research_notes:

Listed 1640/43 New Haven Town Records with four persons and estate of £60. His lot 36 was occupied by Buckingham family from 1639 until late 19th century. narrative_blocks.header: Thomas Hooker trusted him above all others
narrative_blocks.body: In his 1647 will, Rev. Thomas Hooker named his "beloved friend William Goodwin" as overseer of his estate — the man he trusted to protect his widow and children. After Hooker's death, Goodwin married that widow, Susanna Garbrand Hooker, sometime after 1654.

research_notes:
Inherited his father's Hartford dwelling house and home lot plus land. His son John Talcott and grandson John Talcott received bequests in John Sr.'s will. Will specified son John should be settled in either the house John Jr. lived in or John Sr.'s house.
CC NOTE [U00018]: On the night of October 31, 1687, Lt.-Col. John Talcott aided Capt. Joseph Wadsworth (U00018) in seizing the Charter of Connecticut granted by King Charles II and hiding it in the Charter Oak tree in Hartford — preventing its seizure by Governor Sir Edmund Andros. This is one of the most celebrated events in Connecticut colonial history.

birth.notes: Book says 'probably born in Newtown, now Cambridge, about 1634 or 35.' First child of John Talcott born in America — siblings Mary and John were born in England.

research_notes:
b. ca.1634-1635 Newtown (Cambridge) MA; d. Nov 10, 1691 Wethersfield CT. Son of John Talcott (T00011, Hartford founder) and Dorothy Mott (U00011). Talcott Pedigree #36. HARVARD COLLEGE 1658 — graduated and made freeman 1662. "The scholar of the family" — his father's will bequeathed him all books (except the "Martyr Book," given to son John). From 1669-1684: Commissioner for Wethersfield; 1670-1684: Deputy to General Court, acting Secretary in Col. Allyn's absence (Oct 1684). 1676: Standing committee ordering measures between General Court sessions. 1677: Lt. Wethersfield Trained Band; 1679: Lt. Troop; 1681: Captain, Troop of Hartford Co. From 1683: Assistant until death. One of original proprietors of Glastonbury — owned lot purchased by his father in 1643 of Samuel Sherman and Richard Gildersleeve ("44 rods wide, running back from the river three miles to the great wilderness"). "It is still owned by his descendants." Talcott Pedigree (1876): "From these two sons, John and Samuel, are descended ALL of the name of TALCOTT in America." m. (1) Hannah Holyoke (b. Jun 9, 1644) Nov 7, 1661 — 8 children. Hannah died Feb 2, 1677-8. m. (2) Mary [unknown] Aug 6, 1679 — no issue. Mary died Jan 5, 1710-11 age ~73. Will dated Apr 22, 1691; proved Nov 10, 1691.
CC NOTE [H00001]: Capt. Samuel Talcott's wife Hannah Holyoke was the daughter of Capt. Elizur Holyoke and Mary Pynchon — Mary being daughter of Hon. William Pynchon, one of the original patentees of the Massachusetts Bay Colony Charter (1629), and founder of Springfield MA. The Pynchon-Talcott marriage joins the Hartford founding family (Talcott) with the Springfield founding family (Pynchon) in one generation. Note: William Pynchon was the first person prosecuted for heresy in Massachusetts (1650) for his theological treatise on the Atonement — which is why he returned to England 1652. His daughter nevertheless married into the Talcott line, connecting Hartford founders to Massachusetts Bay's most controversial Puritan theologian.
